title: Regression Analysis
text: Regression analysis predicts outcome one predictors. mariposa provides two regression functions SPSS-compatible output: functions support two interface styles: Formula interface: linear_regression(data, y ~ x1 + x2) – standard R style SPSS-style: linear_regression(data, dependent = y, predictors = c(x1, x2)) – familiar SPSS users

title: Regression Analysis
text: Start single predictor: output includes four sections matching SPSS REGRESSION: Model Summary: R, R-squared, Adjusted R-squared ANOVA Table: Overall model significance Coefficients: B (unstandardized), Beta (standardized), t, p, confidence intervals Descriptives: Mean SD variables

title: Regression Analysis
text: B (unstandardized): 1-unit increase predictor, outcome changes B units. intercept predicted value predictors zero. Beta (standardized): Allows comparison across predictors measured different scales. Beta 0.30 means 1-SD increase predictor associated 0.30-SD change outcome. p-value: Values 0.05 indicate predictor’s effect statistically significant.

title: Regression Analysis
text: Add predictors explain variance: Compare Beta values see predictor strongest effect.

title: Regression Analysis
text: prefer SPSS approach separate dependent predictor arguments: Results identical regardless interface use.

title: Regression Analysis
text: Weights treated frequency weights, matching SPSS WEIGHT behavior.

title: Regression Analysis
text: Run separate regressions subgroup: matches SPSS SPLIT FILE – separate model fitted group.

title: Regression Analysis
text: R-squared tells much variance predictors explain: R-squared = 0.01 – 0.05: Small effect R-squared = 0.06 – 0.13: Medium effect R-squared = 0.14+: Large effect benchmarks follow Cohen’s (1988) conventions social sciences. Always check ANOVA table confirm model statistically significant.

title: Regression Analysis
text: Use logistic_regression() outcome binary. First, create binary variable:
code: survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(high_satisfaction = ifelse(life_satisfaction >= 4, 1, 0))

title: Regression Analysis
text: output includes five sections matching SPSS LOGISTIC REGRESSION: Omnibus Test: overall model significant? Model Summary: -2 Log Likelihood pseudo R-squared values Hosmer-Lemeshow Test: model fit data well? Classification Table: well model classify cases? Coefficients: B, Wald, Exp(B) (odds ratios), confidence intervals

title: Regression Analysis
text: key statistic logistic regression Exp(B), odds ratio: Exp(B) > 1: unit increase predictor increases odds outcome Exp(B) < 1: unit increase predictor decreases odds Exp(B) = 1: effect example, Exp(B) = 1.50 means odds increase 50% 1-unit increase predictor. Exp(B) = 0.80 means odds decrease 20%.

title: Regression Analysis
Regression Analysis
Regression Analysis
Regression Analysis
Regression Analysis
text: Pseudo R-squared values directly comparable linear regression R-squared, provide rough indication model quality: Nagelkerke R-squared: Adjusted reach 1.0, commonly reported Cox & Snell R-squared: reach 1.0, always lower McFadden R-squared: Values 0.20 indicate good fit Hosmer-Lemeshow Test: non-significant result (p>.05p > .05) means model fits well. significant result suggests model may adequately describe data. Classification Table: Shows percentage cases correctly predicted. Compare base rate (percentage larger group) – model better simply guessing common outcome.

title: Regression Analysis
text: full regression analysis workflow:

title: Regression Analysis
text: Check correlations first. Use pearson_cor() explore bivariate relationships building regression model. Examine descriptives. Use describe() check variable distributions, ranges, missing data running regression. Compare Beta values. multiple regression, standardized Beta coefficients tell predictor strongest effect, regardless measurement scale. Report completely. Always include R-squared, F-test (linear) Omnibus test (logistic), individual coefficients, sample size. Watch multicollinearity. predictors highly correlated , coefficients may unstable. Check bivariate correlations interpreting results. Binary outcomes need logistic regression. Never use linear_regression() 0/1 outcome – predicted values can fall outside 0-1, statistical tests invalid.

title: Regression Analysis
text: linear_regression() predicts continuous outcomes, providing B, Beta, ANOVA table, R-squared logistic_regression() predicts binary outcomes, providing odds ratios, classification table, pseudo R-squared support formula SPSS-style interfaces, survey weights, grouped analysis Always check correlations descriptives building regression models

title: Regression Analysis
text: Create reliable scale scores first – see vignette("scale-analysis") Compare group means directly – see vignette("hypothesis-testing") Explore bivariate relationships – see vignette("correlation-analysis") Handle survey weights properly – see vignette("survey-weights")

title: Scale Analysis
text: Scale analysis process combining multiple survey items single score reliably measures concept. mariposa, typical workflow : Check reliability reliability() – items measure thing? Explore structure efa() – items group dimensions? Create scores scale_index() – compute mean indices Standardize pomps() – transform comparable 0-100 scale guide walks step using trust items survey_data (trust government, media, science).

title: Scale Analysis
text: creating scale score, check whether items measure concept consistently: output shows Cronbach’s Alpha, item-total correlations, alpha--item-deleted – matching SPSS RELIABILITY output.
code: reliability(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #>  #> Reliability Analysis Results #> ---------------------------- #> - Items: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - N of Items: 3 #>  #> Reliability Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>   Cronbach's Alpha:              0.047 #>   Alpha (standardized):          0.048 #>   N of Items:                    3 #>   N (listwise):                  2135 #>  #> Item Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item  mean    sd    n #>  trust_government 2.621 1.162 2135 #>       trust_media 2.430 1.156 2135 #>     trust_science 3.624 1.034 2135 #>  #> Inter-Item Correlation Matrix: #> ------------------------------  #>                  trust_government trust_media trust_science #> trust_government            1.000       0.014         0.020 #> trust_media                 0.014       1.000         0.015 #> trust_science               0.020       0.015         1.000 #> ------------------------------  #>  #> Item-Total Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item scale_mean_deleted scale_var_deleted corrected_r #>  trust_government               6.05             2.440       0.024 #>       trust_media               6.25             2.467       0.020 #>     trust_science               5.05             2.723       0.025 #>  alpha_deleted #>          0.029 #>          0.040 #>          0.027

title: Scale Analysis
text: Cronbach’s Alpha tells internally consistent scale : Alpha > 0.90: Excellent 0.80 – 0.90: Good 0.70 – 0.80: Acceptable 0.60 – 0.70: Questionable 0.60: Reconsider items Item-Total Correlation shows well item fits: 0.40: Good fit 0.20 – 0.40: Needs review 0.20: Consider removing Alpha Item Deleted shows happens without item. alpha increases remove item, item weakens scale.

title: Scale Analysis
code: reliability(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,             weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Reliability Analysis Results #> ------------------------------------- #> - Items: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - N of Items: 3 #> - Weights: sampling_weight #>  #> Reliability Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>   Cronbach's Alpha:              0.052 #>   Alpha (standardized):          0.053 #>   N of Items:                    3 #>   N (listwise):                  2150.20 (weighted) #>  #> Item Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item  mean    sd      n #>  trust_government 2.621 1.162 2150.2 #>       trust_media 2.434 1.158 2150.2 #>     trust_science 3.624 1.033 2150.2 #>  #> Inter-Item Correlation Matrix: #> ------------------------------  #>                  trust_government trust_media trust_science #> trust_government            1.000       0.017         0.021 #> trust_media                 0.017       1.000         0.017 #> trust_science               0.021       0.017         1.000 #> ------------------------------  #>  #> Item-Total Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item scale_mean_deleted scale_var_deleted corrected_r #>  trust_government               6.06             2.451       0.026 #>       trust_media               6.25             2.469       0.023 #>     trust_science               5.05             2.737       0.027 #>  alpha_deleted #>          0.033 #>          0.041 #>          0.033

title: Scale Analysis
text: items share naming pattern, select efficiently:
code: reliability(survey_data, starts_with("trust")) #>  #> Reliability Analysis Results #> ---------------------------- #> - Items: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - N of Items: 3 #>  #> Reliability Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>   Cronbach's Alpha:              0.047 #>   Alpha (standardized):          0.048 #>   N of Items:                    3 #>   N (listwise):                  2135 #>  #> Item Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item  mean    sd    n #>  trust_government 2.621 1.162 2135 #>       trust_media 2.430 1.156 2135 #>     trust_science 3.624 1.034 2135 #>  #> Inter-Item Correlation Matrix: #> ------------------------------  #>                  trust_government trust_media trust_science #> trust_government            1.000       0.014         0.020 #> trust_media                 0.014       1.000         0.015 #> trust_science               0.020       0.015         1.000 #> ------------------------------  #>  #> Item-Total Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item scale_mean_deleted scale_var_deleted corrected_r #>  trust_government               6.05             2.440       0.024 #>       trust_media               6.25             2.467       0.020 #>     trust_science               5.05             2.723       0.025 #>  alpha_deleted #>          0.029 #>          0.040 #>          0.027

title: Scale Analysis
text: Check whether reliability holds across subgroups:

title: Scale Analysis
text: KMO (Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin) measures whether data suitable factor analysis: 0.80: Good excellent 0.60 – 0.80: Acceptable 0.60: Factor analysis may appropriate Bartlett’s Test significant (p<.05p < .05), confirming correlations exist data. Eigenvalues show much variance component explains. default, components eigenvalue > 1 retained (Kaiser criterion). Factor Loadings show item-component associations: 0.70: Strong 0.40 – 0.70: Moderate 0.40: Suppressed default

title: Scale Analysis
text: Varimax (default) assumes uncorrelated factors – simpler interpret: Oblimin allows correlated factors – often realistic social science: oblimin, get Pattern Matrix (unique contributions) Structure Matrix (total correlations). Promax another oblique rotation, common SPSS. Like oblimin, produces Pattern Structure matrices:

title: Scale Analysis
text: default, efa() uses PCA (Principal Component Analysis). true factor analysis model, use Maximum Likelihood: ML extraction provides goodness--fit test evaluates whether factor model adequately reproduces observed correlations. non-significant chi-square (p>.05p > .05) suggests good fit. ML also reports initial communalities SMC (squared multiple correlations) instead 1.0. can combine extraction rotation:

title: Scale Analysis
text: confirming reliability, create mean index: scale_index() works inside mutate(), computing row-wise mean across items.
code: survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust = scale_index(., trust_government, trust_media, trust_science))  # Check the result survey_data %>%   describe(m_trust) #>  #> Descriptive Statistics #> ---------------------- #>  Variable  Mean Median  SD Range IQR Skewness    N Missing #>   m_trust 2.915      3 0.7     4   1    0.015 2500       0 #> ----------------------------------------

title: Scale Analysis
code: survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust2 = scale_index(., starts_with("trust")))

title: Scale Analysis
text: pick() function works %>% |> pipes:
code: survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust3 = scale_index(     pick(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science)   ))

title: Scale Analysis
text: Require minimum number valid responses per respondent, matching SPSS MEAN.2() syntax: respondent answered fewer 2 items, receive NA instead potentially unreliable score.
code: survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust_strict = scale_index(     ., trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,     min_valid = 2   ))

title: Scale Analysis
text: Transform scores common 0-100 scale cross-scale comparison: score 0 means minimum possible, 100 means maximum. Always specify theoretical scale range consistent results.
code: survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(trust_pomps = pomps(m_trust, scale_min = 1, scale_max = 5))  survey_data %>%   describe(trust_pomps) #>  #> Descriptive Statistics #> ---------------------- #>     Variable   Mean Median   SD Range IQR Skewness    N Missing #>  trust_pomps 47.885     50 17.5   100  25    0.015 2500       0 #> ----------------------------------------

title: Scale Analysis
code: survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(across(     c(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science),     ~ pomps(.x, scale_min = 1, scale_max = 5),     .names = "{.col}_pomps"   ))

title: Scale Analysis
text: typical scale analysis workflow start finish:
code: # 1. Check reliability reliability(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #>  #> Reliability Analysis Results #> ---------------------------- #> - Items: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - N of Items: 3 #>  #> Reliability Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>   Cronbach's Alpha:              0.047 #>   Alpha (standardized):          0.048 #>   N of Items:                    3 #>   N (listwise):                  2135 #>  #> Item Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item  mean    sd    n #>  trust_government 2.621 1.162 2135 #>       trust_media 2.430 1.156 2135 #>     trust_science 3.624 1.034 2135 #>  #> Inter-Item Correlation Matrix: #> ------------------------------  #>                  trust_government trust_media trust_science #> trust_government            1.000       0.014         0.020 #> trust_media                 0.014       1.000         0.015 #> trust_science               0.020       0.015         1.000 #> ------------------------------  #>  #> Item-Total Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item scale_mean_deleted scale_var_deleted corrected_r #>  trust_government               6.05             2.440       0.024 #>       trust_media               6.25             2.467       0.020 #>     trust_science               5.05             2.723       0.025 #>  alpha_deleted #>          0.029 #>          0.040 #>          0.027  # 2. Explore factor structure efa(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #>  #> Exploratory Factor Analysis (PCA, Unrotated) Results #> ---------------------------------------------------- #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - Extraction: Principal Component Analysis #> - Rotation: None #> - N of Factors: 1 #>  #> KMO and Bartlett's Test #> ----------------------------------------  #>   Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin Measure:     0.506 #>   Bartlett's Chi-Square:          3.203 #>   df:                             3 #>   Sig.:                           0.361 #>  #> Communalities #> ----------------------------------------  #>          variable initial extraction #>  trust_government       1      0.356 #>       trust_media       1      0.225 #>     trust_science       1      0.460 #> Extraction Method: Principal Component Analysis. #>  #> Total Variance Explained #> ----------------------------------------  #>   PC1  Eigenvalue: 1.042  Variance: 34.719%  Cumulative: 34.719% #>   PC2  Eigenvalue: 0.992  Variance: 33.056%  Cumulative: 67.775% #>   PC3  Eigenvalue: 0.967  Variance: 32.225%  Cumulative: 100.000% #>  #> Component Matrix (unrotated) #> ----------------------------------------  #>                      PC1 #> trust_science     -0.678 #> trust_government  -0.597 #> trust_media       -0.475 #> Extraction Method: Principal Component Analysis.  # 3. Create mean index (Alpha was acceptable) survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust = scale_index(., trust_government, trust_media, trust_science))  # 4. Transform to POMPS for reporting survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(trust_pomps = pomps(m_trust, scale_min = 1, scale_max = 5))  # 5. Use in further analyses survey_data %>%   t_test(m_trust, group = region, weights = sampling_weight) #> Weighted t-Test Results #> ----------------------- #>  #> - Grouping variable: region #> - Groups compared: East vs. West #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #> - Null hypothesis (mu): 0.000 #>  #>  #> --- m_trust --- #>  #>   East: mean = 2.905, n = 509.0 #>   West: mean = 2.919, n = 2007.0 #>  #> Weighted t-test Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>         Assumption t_stat       df p_value mean_diff        conf_int sig #>    Equal variances -0.400 2514.000   0.689    -0.014 [-0.082, 0.054]     #>  Unequal variances -0.406  799.225   0.685    -0.014 [-0.081, 0.053]     #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Effect Sizes: #> ------------  #>  Variable Cohens_d Hedges_g Glass_Delta Effect_Size #>   m_trust    -0.02    -0.02       -0.02  negligible #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation: #> - Cohen's d: pooled standard deviation (classic) #> - Hedges' g: bias-corrected Cohen's d (preferred) #> - Glass' Delta: control group standard deviation only #> - Small effect: |effect| ~ 0.2 #> - Medium effect: |effect| ~ 0.5 #> - Large effect: |effect| ~ 0.8

title: Scale Analysis
text: Always check reliability first. mean index unreliable items produces meaningless results. Aim Alpha > .70. Use min_valid wisely. scales 3-5 items, min_valid = 2 reasonable compromise data loss score quality. Specify scale_min scale_max pomps(). Using observed values instead theoretical range makes scores incomparable across samples. Run EFA many items. 6+ items, factor analysis can reveal unexpected dimensions create indices. Check grouped reliability. Alpha may differ substantially across subgroups (e.g., regions, education levels). scale works well overall might unreliable specific groups.

title: Scale Analysis
text: reliability() checks whether items form consistent scale (Cronbach’s Alpha) efa() discovers underlying dimensions items (Principal Component Analysis) scale_index() computes mean indices use analyses pomps() transforms scores comparable 0-100 scale

title: Scale Analysis
text: Use new scale scores group comparisons – see vignette("hypothesis-testing") Explore relationships scales – see vignette("correlation-analysis") Predict outcomes regression – see vignette("regression-analysis") Apply survey weights analyses – see vignette("survey-weights")

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: Survey weights adjust analysis make results representative target population. Without weights, results describe sample — might biased groups - -represented. Common reasons weighting: Oversampling: Deliberately surveying people small groups ensure adequate sample sizes Non-response: groups less likely participate, creating bias Post-stratification: Adjusting sample match known population demographics mariposa, adding weights simple including weights = sampling_weight function call.

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: Let’s see difference weights make: difference weighted unweighted results tells biased sample . Larger differences mean weights important work.
code: # Without weights (describes the sample) unweighted_stats <- survey_data %>%   summarise(     mean_age = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),     mean_satisfaction = mean(life_satisfaction, na.rm = TRUE)   ) print(unweighted_stats) #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>   mean_age mean_satisfaction #>      <dbl>             <dbl> #> 1     50.5              3.63  # With weights (represents the population) age_weighted <- w_mean(survey_data, age, weights = sampling_weight) satisfaction_weighted <- w_mean(survey_data, life_satisfaction,                                 weights = sampling_weight)  weighted_stats <- data.frame(   mean_age = age_weighted$results$weighted_mean,   mean_satisfaction = satisfaction_weighted$results$weighted_mean ) print(weighted_stats) #>   mean_age mean_satisfaction #> 1  50.5144          3.624863

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: mariposa provides specialized w_* functions individual weighted statistics.

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: Weighting reduces statistical precision estimates. effective sample size (neffn_{eff}) tells much information weighted sample actually carries: neff=(∑wi)2∑wi2n_{eff} = \frac{\left(\sum w_i\right)^2}{\sum w_i^2} design effect ratio actual effective sample size: design effect 1.0 means weights impact precision. Values 1 mean effective sample smaller actual sample — standard errors larger without weights.
code:
actual_n <- nrow(survey_data) age_weighted <- w_mean(survey_data, age, weights = sampling_weight) effective_n <- age_weighted$results$effective_n  cat("Actual sample size:", actual_n, "
") #> Actual sample size: 2500 cat("Effective sample size:", round(effective_n), "
") #> Effective sample size: 2469 cat("Design effect:", round(actual_n / effective_n, 2), "
") #> Design effect: 1.01

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: main analysis functions support weights argument:

title: Working with Survey Weights
code: survey_data %>%   t_test(income, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight) #> Weighted t-Test Results #> ----------------------- #>  #> - Grouping variable: gender #> - Groups compared: Male vs. Female #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #> - Null hypothesis (mu): 0.000 #>  #>  #> --- income --- #>  #>   Male: mean = 3766.999, n = 1048.0 #>   Female: mean = 3721.379, n = 1153.0 #>  #> Weighted t-test Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>         Assumption t_stat       df p_value mean_diff           conf_int sig #>    Equal variances  0.751 2199.000   0.453     45.62 [-73.570, 164.809]     #>  Unequal variances  0.751 2178.884   0.453     45.62 [-73.574, 164.813]     #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Effect Sizes: #> ------------  #>  Variable Cohens_d Hedges_g Glass_Delta Effect_Size #>    income    0.032    0.032       0.032  negligible #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation: #> - Cohen's d: pooled standard deviation (classic) #> - Hedges' g: bias-corrected Cohen's d (preferred) #> - Glass' Delta: control group standard deviation only #> - Small effect: |effect| ~ 0.2 #> - Medium effect: |effect| ~ 0.5 #> - Large effect: |effect| ~ 0.8

title: Working with Survey Weights
code: survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education,                weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted One-Way ANOVA Results #> ------------------------------ #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Null hypothesis: All group means are equal #>   Alternative hypothesis: At least one group mean differs #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Weighted Descriptive Statistics by Group: #>   Basic Secondary: mean = 3.208, sd = 1.243, n = 815.7 #>   Intermediate Secondary: mean = 3.698, sd = 1.109, n = 629.6 #>   Academic Secondary: mean = 3.851, sd = 0.996, n = 618.2 #>   University: mean = 4.040, sd = 0.961, n = 373.2 #>  #> Weighted ANOVA Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>          Source Sum_Squares   df Mean_Square      F p_value sig #>  Between Groups     241.130    3      80.377 65.359   <.001   1 #>   Within Groups    2992.019 2433        1.23                    #>           Total    3233.149 2436                                #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Assumption Tests: #> ----------------  #>  Assumption Statistic df1  df2 p_value sig #>       Welch    62.636   3 1216   <.001 *** #>  #> Effect Sizes: #> ------------  #>           Variable Eta_Squared Epsilon_Squared Omega_Squared Effect_Size #>  life_satisfaction       0.075           0.073         0.073      medium #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation: #> - Eta-squared: Proportion of variance explained (biased upward) #> - Epsilon-squared: Less biased than eta-squared #> - Omega-squared: Unbiased estimate (preferred for publication) #> - Small effect: eta-squared ~ 0.01, Medium effect: eta-squared ~ 0.06, Large effect: eta-squared ~ 0.14 #>  #> Post-hoc tests: Use tukey_test() for pairwise comparisons

title: Working with Survey Weights
code: survey_data %>%   chi_square(education, employment, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Chi-Squared Test of Independence  #> ------------------------------------------ #>  #> - Variables: education × employment #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #>  #> Observed Frequencies: #>                       employment #> education              Student Employed Unemployed Retired Other #>   Basic Secondary            0      573         66     175    34 #>   Intermediate Seco...       0      420         52     139    29 #>   Academic Secondary        46      370         45     149    33 #>   University                34      240         21      72    20 #>  #> Expected Frequencies: #>                       employment #> education              Student Employed Unemployed Retired  Other #>   Basic Secondary       26.942  539.851     61.967 180.175 39.066 #>   Intermediate Seco...  20.334  407.434     46.767 135.981 29.484 #>   Academic Secondary    20.429  409.344     46.986 136.618 29.622 #>   University            12.295  246.371     28.280  82.226 17.828 #>  #> Chi-Squared Test Results: #> --------------------------------------------------  #>  Chi_squared df p_value sig #>      130.696 12   <.001 *** #> --------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Effect Sizes: #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------  #>     Measure  Value p_value sig Interpretation #>  Cramer's V  0.132   <.001 ***          Small #>       Gamma -0.062   0.122               Weak #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------  #> Table size: 4×5 | N = 2518 #> Note: Phi coefficient only shown for 2x2 tables #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

title: Working with Survey Weights
code: survey_data %>%   pearson_cor(age, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Pearson Correlation  #> ----------------------------- #>  #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #> - Missing data handling: pairwise deletion #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>  #>  #> --- age × income --- #>  #>   Correlation: r = -0.005 #>   Effect size: r²: 0.000 #>   Sample size: n = 2201 #>   95% CI: [-0.046, 0.037] #>   p-value: 0.8276 #>   Significance: ns #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Correlation Strength Interpretation: #>   |r| < 0.30:        Weak correlation #>   0.30 ≤ |r| < 0.70: Moderate correlation #>   |r| ≥ 0.70:        Strong correlation #>  #> r² represents the proportion of variance explained

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: functions work seamlessly group_by():

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: Weights large small can destabilize estimates. Check distribution weights: rule thumb: weights typically range 0.2 5. Extreme values (0.1 10) may indicate problems weighting scheme.
code: weight_stats <- survey_data %>%   summarise(     min_weight = min(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE),     max_weight = max(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE),     mean_weight = mean(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE),     sd_weight = sd(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE)   ) print(weight_stats) #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   min_weight max_weight mean_weight sd_weight #>        <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1      0.702       1.40        1.01     0.113

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: Check many cases lack weight values:
code: missing_weights <- sum(is.na(survey_data$sampling_weight)) total_cases <- nrow(survey_data) cat("Cases with missing weights:", missing_weights, "/", total_cases,     "(", round(missing_weights / total_cases * 100, 1), "%)\n") #> Cases with missing weights: 0 / 2500 ( 0 %)

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: weights extreme, consider trimming reduce variance cost small increase bias:
code: survey_data_trimmed <- survey_data %>%   mutate(     weight_trimmed = case_when(       sampling_weight > 5 ~ 5,       sampling_weight < 0.2 ~ 0.2,       TRUE ~ sampling_weight     )   )  original <- w_mean(survey_data, income, weights = sampling_weight) trimmed <- w_mean(survey_data_trimmed, income, weights = weight_trimmed)  cat("Original weighted mean:", round(original$results$weighted_mean, 1), "\n") #> Original weighted mean: 3743.1 cat("Trimmed weighted mean:", round(trimmed$results$weighted_mean, 1), "\n") #> Trimmed weighted mean: 3743.1

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: Always use weights provided. ensure results generalize population, just sample. Report weighted unweighted results transparency: Document weight source. Explain weights calculated adjust . Report effective sample size. tells readers precision estimates. Check extreme weights. Large variation weights inflates standard errors reduces power.
code: comparison <- data.frame(   Statistic = c("Mean Age", "Mean Income"),   Unweighted = c(     mean(survey_data$age, na.rm = TRUE),     mean(survey_data$income, na.rm = TRUE)   ),   Weighted = c(     w_mean(survey_data, age, weights = sampling_weight)$results$weighted_mean,     w_mean(survey_data, income, weights = sampling_weight)$results$weighted_mean   ) ) print(comparison) #>     Statistic Unweighted  Weighted #> 1    Mean Age    50.5496   50.5144 #> 2 Mean Income  3753.9341 3743.0994

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: full weighted analysis workflow:
code: # 1. Inspect weight properties weight_summary <- survey_data %>%   summarise(     n = n(),     mean = mean(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE),     sd = sd(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE),     min = min(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE),     max = max(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE)   ) print(weight_summary) #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>       n  mean    sd   min   max #>   <int> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1  2500  1.01 0.113 0.702  1.40  # 2. Compare weighted vs unweighted vars_to_check <- c("age", "income", "life_satisfaction") for (var in vars_to_check) {   unw <- mean(survey_data[[var]], na.rm = TRUE)   w_result <- w_mean(survey_data, !!sym(var), weights = sampling_weight)   w <- w_result$results$weighted_mean   cat(sprintf("%s: unweighted = %.2f, weighted = %.2f (diff = %.1f%%)\n",               var, unw, w, (w - unw) / unw * 100)) } #> age: unweighted = 50.55, weighted = 50.51 (diff = -0.1%) #> income: unweighted = 3753.93, weighted = 3743.10 (diff = -0.3%) #> life_satisfaction: unweighted = 3.63, weighted = 3.62 (diff = -0.1%)  # 3. Weighted group comparisons survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   describe(income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Descriptive Statistics #> ------------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = East #> -------------------- #> ---------------------------------------- #>  Variable     Mean Median       SD Range  IQR Skewness Effective_N #>    income 3760.687   3600 1388.321  7200 1700    0.718       421.9 #> ---------------------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = West #> -------------------- #> ---------------------------------------- #>  Variable     Mean Median       SD Range  IQR Skewness Effective_N #>    income 3738.586   3500 1433.325  7200 1900    0.726      1738.1 #> ----------------------------------------  # 4. Weighted hypothesis test survey_data %>%   t_test(income, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight) #> Weighted t-Test Results #> ----------------------- #>  #> - Grouping variable: gender #> - Groups compared: Male vs. Female #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #> - Null hypothesis (mu): 0.000 #>  #>  #> --- income --- #>  #>   Male: mean = 3766.999, n = 1048.0 #>   Female: mean = 3721.379, n = 1153.0 #>  #> Weighted t-test Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>         Assumption t_stat       df p_value mean_diff           conf_int sig #>    Equal variances  0.751 2199.000   0.453     45.62 [-73.570, 164.809]     #>  Unequal variances  0.751 2178.884   0.453     45.62 [-73.574, 164.813]     #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Effect Sizes: #> ------------  #>  Variable Cohens_d Hedges_g Glass_Delta Effect_Size #>    income    0.032    0.032       0.032  negligible #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation: #> - Cohen's d: pooled standard deviation (classic) #> - Hedges' g: bias-corrected Cohen's d (preferred) #> - Glass' Delta: control group standard deviation only #> - Small effect: |effect| ~ 0.2 #> - Medium effect: |effect| ~ 0.5 #> - Large effect: |effect| ~ 0.8

what: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
text: Professional statistical analysis survey data R. mariposa (Marburg Initiative Political Social Analysis) provides 44 statistical functions analyzing survey data. functions support survey weights, grouped analysis via dplyr::group_by(), produce publication-ready output. Results validated SPSS v29 full reproducibility (4,986+ tests pass).

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
code: # Install from GitHub devtools::install_github("YannickDiehl/mariposa")

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
code: library(mariposa) library(dplyr)  # Load example survey data (2,500 respondents) data(survey_data)  # Descriptive statistics with survey weights survey_data %>%   describe(age, income, life_satisfaction, weights = sampling_weight)  # Frequency table survey_data %>%   frequency(education, weights = sampling_weight)  # Compare groups with t-test survey_data %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight)  # Scale analysis workflow reliability(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science)  survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust = scale_index(., trust_government, trust_media, trust_science))  # Regression survey_data %>%   linear_regression(life_satisfaction ~ age + income, weights = sampling_weight)

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
text: Every function handles survey weights correctly:
code: # Weighted mean, median, SD survey_data %>%   w_mean(age, income, weights = sampling_weight)  # Grouped weighted analysis survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   describe(satisfaction, weights = sampling_weight)

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
text: Full support pipes grouped operations:
code: survey_data %>%   filter(age >= 18) %>%   group_by(region) %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight)

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
code: # Factorial ANOVA with Type III SS survey_data %>%   factorial_anova(dv = income, between = c(gender, education),                   weights = sampling_weight)  # ANCOVA with covariate adjustment survey_data %>%   ancova(dv = income, between = gender, covariate = age,          weights = sampling_weight)

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
code: # Parametric: ANOVA → Tukey/Scheffe result <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education, weights = sampling_weight)  result %>% tukey_test()    # Pairwise comparisons result %>% levene_test()   # Variance homogeneity  # Non-parametric: Kruskal-Wallis → Dunn kw_result <- survey_data %>%   kruskal_wallis(life_satisfaction, group = education)  kw_result %>% dunn_test()  # Pairwise Dunn comparisons

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
text: Every function validated SPSS v29 across four scenarios: weighted/unweighted grouped/ungrouped. ’re migrating SPSS, results match: SPSS: mariposa:
code: WEIGHT BY sampling_weight. T-TEST GROUPS=gender(1 2)   /VARIABLES=satisfaction. survey_data %>%   t_test(satisfaction, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight)

title: Analysis of Covariance: ANCOVA — ancova
text: ancova() tests whether group means differ controlling one continuous covariates. performs factorial ANCOVA using Type III Sum Squares, matching SPSS UNIANOVA output keyword. Think : ANOVA removes effect confounding variables first Testing group differences "adjusted" means Combining regression (covariates) ANOVA (factors) one model test tells : Whether factor significant effect controlling covariates relationship covariate outcome Effect sizes factors covariates (partial eta squared) Estimated marginal means (group means adjusted covariates)

title: Analysis of Covariance: ANCOVA — ancova

title: Analysis of Covariance: ANCOVA — ancova

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof
text: chisq_gof() tests whether observed frequencies categorical variable match expected frequencies. default, tests equal proportions (uniform distribution). can also specify custom expected proportions. Think : Testing whether categories equally distributed Comparing observed distribution theoretical distribution one-sample version chi-square test test tells : Whether observed frequencies differ expected frequencies strong deviation (chi-square statistic) frequency table observed, expected, residual counts

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof
text: P-value: p < 0.05, distribution differs expected p < 0.001: strong evidence distribution differs p < 0.01: Strong evidence distribution differs p < 0.05: Moderate evidence distribution differs p >= 0.05: significant deviation expected distribution Residuals: difference observed expected counts. Large positive residuals indicate category cases expected; large negative residuals indicate fewer cases expected.

(Default: TRUE) digits Decimal places percentages (Default: 1)

text: describe() gives complete summary numeric variables - like age, income, satisfaction scores. first step analysis, helping understand typical, unusual, spread data . Think health check data reveals: average value? middle value? spread responses? unusual patterns outliers?

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
code: describe(   data,   ...,   weights = NULL,   show = "short",   probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75),   na.rm = TRUE,   excess = TRUE )

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables want summarize. List separated commas, use helpers like starts_with("trust") weights Optional survey weights population-representative results. Without weights, describe sample. weights, describe population. show statistics display: "short" (default): Essential stats (mean, median, SD, range, IQR, skewness) "": Everything including variance, kurtosis, mode, quantiles Custom list: Choose specific stats like c("mean", "sd", "range") probs quantiles, percentiles show (default: 25th, 50th, 75th) na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE) excess kurtosis, show excess kurtosis? (Default: TRUE, easier interpret)

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
text: summary table descriptive statistics variable

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
text: Key statistics tell : n: many valid responses (watch many missing) Mean: average value Median: middle value (half , half ) SD: Standard deviation - spread values Range: minimum maximum values IQR: Interquartile range - middle 50% values Skewness: Whether data leans left (negative) right (positive) Kurtosis: Whether unusual outliers

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
text: Always start ! Use describe() : Check data quality (impossible values?) Understand distributions testing Spot outliers might affect analyses Compare groups side side

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
text: Mean ≈ Median: Data roughly symmetric Mean > Median: Right-skewed (tail extends right) Mean < Median: Left-skewed (tail extends left) Large SD: Responses vary widely Small SD: Responses similar

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe

what: Calculate kurtosis using the SPSS sample-corrected formula — .calc_kurtosis
title: Calculate kurtosis using the SPSS sample-corrected formula — .calc_kurtosis
text: Uses Type 2 (sample excess kurtosis) formula matching SPSS FREQUENCIES output. unweighted data: G2 = ((n+1)g2 + 6) * (n-1) / ((n-2)(n-3)) g2 = m4/m2^2 - 3 (population excess kurtosis). weighted data: Uses frequency-weighted N = sum(w) place n. Reference: Joanes & Gill (1998), "Comparing measures sample skewness kurtosis"

what: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: dunn_test() tells exactly groups different Kruskal-Wallis finds overall differences. non-parametric equivalent Tukey Scheffe post-hoc test. Think : Kruskal-Wallis says "differences somewhere" Dunn test says "specifically, Group differs Group C" way make possible pairwise comparisons using rank-based statistics

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
code: dunn_test(x, ...)  # Default S3 method dunn_test(x, ...)

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: x Kruskal-Wallis results kruskal_wallis() ... Additional arguments passed methods. method kruskal_wallis objects accepts p_adjust (character): method adjusting p-values multiple comparisons. Options: "bonferroni" (default, conservative), "holm", "BH", "hochberg", "hommel", "", "fdr", "none".

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: Pairwise comparison results showing: group pairs significantly different Z-statistics based rank differences Adjusted p-values (controlling multiple comparisons)

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: Z-Statistics: Based differences mean ranks groups Large absolute Z values indicate big rank differences Positive Z: First group higher mean rank second Negative Z: Second group higher mean rank first Adjusted P-values: Control multiple comparisons p < 0.05: Groups significantly different p >= 0.05: significant difference groups

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: pair groups (, j): $$Z_{ij} = \frac{\bar{R}_i - \bar{R}_j}{\sqrt{\frac{N(N+1)}{12}   \left(\frac{1}{n_i} + \frac{1}{n_j}\right)}}$$ \(\bar{R}_i\) mean rank group , \(N\) total sample size, \(n_i\) size group .

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: Bonferroni (default): conservative, multiplies p number comparisons Holm: Step-method, less conservative Bonferroni BH: Controls false discovery rate, good many comparisons

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: Use Dunn test : Kruskal-Wallis test shows significant differences (p < 0.05) want know specific groups differ data violates normality assumptions ordinal data skewed distributions

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: Non-parametric equivalent tukey_test() scheffe_test() Follow-kruskal_wallis(), just like Tukey follows ANOVA Uses ranks instead raw values, making robust outliers

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: Dunn, O. J. (1964). Multiple comparisons using rank sums. Technometrics, 6(3), 241-252.

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Perform Kruskal-Wallis followed by Dunn post-hoc test kw_result <- survey_data %>%   kruskal_wallis(life_satisfaction, group = education)  # Dunn post-hoc comparisons (default: Bonferroni) kw_result %>% dunn_test() #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) Results #> --------------------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - P-value adjustment: Bonferroni #>  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                 Group 1                Group 2       Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>         Basic Secondary Intermediate Secondary  -7.402     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary     Academic Secondary  -9.465     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary             University -11.159     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  Intermediate Secondary     Academic Secondary  -1.973     0.048   0.291      #>  Intermediate Secondary             University  -4.539     <.001   <.001 ***  #>      Academic Secondary             University  -2.790     0.005   0.032   *  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive Z: First group has higher mean rank #> - Negative Z: First group has lower mean rank #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons  # With Holm correction (less conservative) kw_result %>% dunn_test(p_adjust = "holm") #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Holm) Results #> --------------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - P-value adjustment: Holm #>  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                 Group 1                Group 2       Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>         Basic Secondary Intermediate Secondary  -7.402     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary     Academic Secondary  -9.465     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary             University -11.159     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  Intermediate Secondary     Academic Secondary  -1.973     0.048   0.048   *  #>  Intermediate Secondary             University  -4.539     <.001   <.001 ***  #>      Academic Secondary             University  -2.790     0.005   0.011   *  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive Z: First group has higher mean rank #> - Negative Z: First group has lower mean rank #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons  # With Benjamini-Hochberg (controls false discovery rate) kw_result %>% dunn_test(p_adjust = "BH") #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Benjamini-Hochberg) Results #> ----------------------------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - P-value adjustment: Benjamini-Hochberg #>  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                 Group 1                Group 2       Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>         Basic Secondary Intermediate Secondary  -7.402     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary     Academic Secondary  -9.465     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary             University -11.159     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  Intermediate Secondary     Academic Secondary  -1.973     0.048   0.048   *  #>  Intermediate Secondary             University  -4.539     <.001   <.001 ***  #>      Academic Secondary             University  -2.790     0.005   0.006  **  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive Z: First group has higher mean rank #> - Negative Z: First group has lower mean rank #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons  # With weights kw_weighted <- survey_data %>%   kruskal_wallis(life_satisfaction, group = education,                  weights = sampling_weight)  kw_weighted %>% dunn_test() #> Weighted Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) Results #> ------------------------------------------------ #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #> - P-value adjustment: Bonferroni #>  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                 Group 1                Group 2       Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>         Basic Secondary Intermediate Secondary  -7.351     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary     Academic Secondary  -9.444     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary             University -10.927     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  Intermediate Secondary     Academic Secondary  -2.007     0.045   0.269      #>  Intermediate Secondary             University  -4.484     <.001   <.001 ***  #>      Academic Secondary             University  -2.735     0.006   0.037   *  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive Z: First group has higher mean rank #> - Negative Z: First group has lower mean rank #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons  # Multiple variables kw_multi <- survey_data %>%   kruskal_wallis(life_satisfaction, trust_government,                  group = education)  kw_multi %>% dunn_test() #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) Results #> --------------------------------------- #>  #> - Grouping variable: education #> - P-value adjustment: Bonferroni #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                 Group 1                Group 2       Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>         Basic Secondary Intermediate Secondary  -7.402     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary     Academic Secondary  -9.465     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary             University -11.159     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  Intermediate Secondary     Academic Secondary  -1.973     0.048   0.291      #>  Intermediate Secondary             University  -4.539     <.001   <.001 ***  #>      Academic Secondary             University  -2.790     0.005   0.032   *  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> --- trust_government --- #>  #> ---------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                 Group 1                Group 2      Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>         Basic Secondary Intermediate Secondary  0.955     0.340   1.000      #>         Basic Secondary     Academic Secondary  0.552     0.581   1.000      #>         Basic Secondary             University -0.037     0.971   1.000      #>  Intermediate Secondary     Academic Secondary -0.378     0.706   1.000      #>  Intermediate Secondary             University -0.821     0.411   1.000      #>      Academic Secondary             University -0.490     0.624   1.000      #> ---------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - what: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
text: efa() performs Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) discover underlying patterns survey items. Supports Principal Component Analysis (PCA) Maximum Likelihood (ML) extraction. R equivalent SPSS's FACTOR procedure. example, 6 items measuring different attitudes, EFA can reveal whether group 2-3 underlying dimensions (factors components).

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
code: efa(   data,   ...,   n_factors = NULL,   rotation = "varimax",   extraction = "pca",   weights = NULL,   use = "pairwise",   sort = TRUE,   blank = 0.4,   na.rm = TRUE )

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... items analyze. Use bare column names separated commas, tidyselect helpers like starts_with("trust"). n_factors Number components extract. Default NULL uses Kaiser criterion (eigenvalue > 1). rotation Rotation method: "varimax" (default, orthogonal), "oblimin" (oblique, allows correlated factors), "promax" (oblique, power-based), "none". extraction Extraction method: "pca" (default, Principal Component Analysis) "ml" (Maximum Likelihood, enables goodness--fit testing, assumes multivariate normality). weights Optional survey weights population-representative results. use handle missing data correlation computation: "pairwise" (default, matches SPSS) "complete" (listwise). sort Logical. Sort loadings size within component? Default TRUE. blank Numeric. Suppress (hide) loadings absolute value threshold print output. Default 0.40 (matches SPSS BLANK(.40)). Set 0 show loadings. na.rm Logical. Remove missing values? Default TRUE.

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
text: efa result object containing: loadings Component loading matrix (rotated rotation applied) unrotated_loadings Unrotated component matrix eigenvalues eigenvalues correlation matrix variance_explained Tibble Total, % Variance, Cumulative % rotation_variance Tibble rotation sums squared loadings communalities Extraction communalities variable kmo List overall KMO per-item MSA values bartlett List chi_sq, df, p_value rotation Rotation method used extraction Extraction method used n_factors Number components extracted correlation_matrix Correlation matrix used analysis initial_communalities Initial communalities (1.0 PCA, SMC ML) goodness_of_fit Goodness--fit test (ML ): chi_sq, df, p_value. NULL PCA. uniquenesses Unique variances per variable (ML , NULL PCA) pattern_matrix Pattern matrix (oblimin/promax , NULL otherwise) structure_matrix Structure matrix (oblimin/promax , NULL otherwise) factor_correlations Factor correlation matrix (oblimin/promax , NULL otherwise) variables Character vector variable names weights Weights variable name NULL n Sample size col_prefix Column name prefix: "PC" PCA, "Factor" ML sort Whether loadings sorted blank Suppression threshold

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
text: KMO (Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin) measures sampling adequacy: KMO > 0.90: Marvelous KMO 0.80 - 0.90: Meritorious KMO 0.70 - 0.80: Middling KMO 0.60 - 0.70: Mediocre KMO 0.50 - 0.60: Miserable KMO < 0.50: Unacceptable - use factor analysis Bartlett's Test Sphericity tests whether correlations significantly different zero. significant result (p < .05) means correlation matrix suitable factor analysis. Eigenvalues indicate much variance component explains. Kaiser criterion retains components eigenvalue > 1. Factor Loadings show strongly item relates component: |loading| > 0.70: Strong association |loading| 0.40 - 0.70: Moderate association |loading| < 0.40: Weak (suppressed default) Communalities show much item's variance explained extracted components. Low communalities (< 0.40) suggest item fit well others.

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
text: PCA (default): Extracts components explaining maximum total variance. Simple robust. assume normality. ML: Extracts factors explaining shared variance . Assumes multivariate normality. Provides goodness--fit test evaluate model fit. non-significant chi-square (p > .05) suggests adequate fit.

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
text: Varimax (default): Assumes factors uncorrelated. Produces simpler, easier--interpret results. Oblimin: Allows factors correlated. realistic social science data. Produces Pattern Matrix (unique contributions) Structure Matrix (total correlations). Promax: Oblique rotation based power transformation Varimax results. Like Oblimin, produces Pattern Structure matrices. Common alternative Oblimin SPSS. None: rotation. Rarely useful interpretation.

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa

what: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: factorial_anova() tests whether group means differ across two factors simultaneously, including interactions. performs factorial (two-way, three-way) ANOVA using Type III Sum Squares, matching SPSS UNIANOVA output. Think : Testing multiple grouping variables Detecting interaction effects (factor combinations matter?) extension one-way ANOVA multiple factors test tells : Whether factor main effect outcome Whether factors interact (effect one depends ) much variance factor explains (partial eta squared)

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
code: factorial_anova(data, dv, between, weights = NULL, ss_type = 3)

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) dv numeric dependent variable analyze (unquoted) Character vector unquoted variable names specifying -subjects factors (2-3 factors). must categorical variables (factor, character, labelled numeric). weights Optional survey weights population-representative results (unquoted variable name) ss_type Type Sum Squares: 3 (default, SPSS standard) 2. Type III recommended unbalanced designs.

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: object class "factorial_anova" containing: anova_table Tibble Source, SS, df, MS, F, p, Partial Eta Squared descriptives Tibble cell means, SDs, Ns factor combination levene_test Tibble Levene's test results (F, df1, df2, p) r_squared R-squared Adjusted R-squared model underlying model object S3 dispatch call_info List metadata (dv, factors, weighted, n_total, n_missing)

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: Main Effects: factor independently affect outcome? Significant main effect = group means differ factor Example: Education affects income regardless gender Interaction Effects: effect one factor depend another? Significant interaction = pattern differs across factor combinations Example: gender gap income varies education level Partial Eta Squared (Effect Size): Less 0.01: Negligible 0.01 0.06: Small 0.06 0.14: Medium 0.14 greater: Large

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: Type III SS tests effect adjusting effects. standard SPSS recommended unbalanced designs (unequal cell sizes). uses orthogonal contrasts (contr.sum) internally.

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: Use factorial ANOVA : one numeric outcome variable 2-3 categorical grouping factors want test main effects interactions data approximately normally distributed within cells

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: ANOVA significant: Check effects significant (main effects vs. interactions) Use tukey_test() post-hoc comparisons main effects Examine cell means interpret interaction patterns Consider effect sizes practical significance

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical Power Analysis Behavioral Sciences (2nd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum Associates. IBM Corp. (2023). IBM SPSS Statistics 29 Algorithms. IBM Corporation. Maxwell, S. E., & Delaney, H. D. (2004). Designing Experiments Analyzing Data (2nd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum Associates.

Dependent variable: income #> - Factors: gender x education #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Family-wise error rate controlled using Tukey HSD #>  #>  #> --- Factor: gender --- #>  #> ----------------------------------------------------  #>   Comparison Difference Lower CI Upper CI p-value Sig #>  Female-Male     -42.32 -162.639   77.999    0.49     #> ----------------------------------------------------  #>  #> --- Factor: education --- #>  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                                 Comparison Difference Lower CI Upper CI p-value #>     Intermediate Secondary-Basic Secondary    833.471  671.079  995.862   <.001 #>         Academic Secondary-Basic Secondary   1465.040 1302.648 1627.432   <.001 #>                 University-Basic Secondary   2578.135 2392.167 2764.104   <.001 #>  Academic Secondary-Intermediate Secondary    631.569  457.746  805.393   <.001 #>          University-Intermediate Secondary   1744.665 1548.634 1940.695   <.001 #>              University-Academic Secondary   1113.096  917.065 1309.126   <.001 #>  Sig #>  *** #>  *** #>  *** #>  *** #>  *** #>  *** #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive differences: First group > Second group #> - Negative differences: First group < Second group #> - Confidence intervals not containing 0 indicate significant differences #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons (family-wise error control) result %>% levene_test() #>  #> Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance  #> ------------------------------------------ #>  #> - Grouping variable: gender * education #> - Center: mean #>  #>  #> --- income --- #>  #> Levene's Test Results: #> -----------------------------------------------------------  #>  Variable F_statistic df1  df2 p_value sig        Conclusion #>    income      44.988   7 2178       0 *** Variances unequal #> -----------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - p > 0.05: Variances are homogeneous (equal variances assumed) #> - p <= 0.05: Variances are heterogeneous (equal variances NOT assumed) #>  #> Recommendation based on Levene test: #> - Use Welch's t-test (unequal variances)

what: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: fisher_test() performs Fisher's exact test independence contingency table. Use instead chi_square() sample small expected cell frequencies 5. Think : exact alternative chi-square test independence Best small samples chi-square approximation may inaccurate SPSS automatically reports Fisher's Exact Test 2x2 tables test tells : Whether two categorical variables related (exact p-value) contingency table observed frequencies Results valid even small samples

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
code: fisher_test(data, row, col, weights = NULL, ...)

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) row row variable (categorical) col column variable (categorical) weights Optional survey weights population-representative results ... Additional arguments (currently unused)

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: Test results showing whether two categorical variables related, including: Exact p-value (two-sided) Contingency table observed frequencies Sample size (N)

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: P-value: p < 0.05, variables likely related (independent) p < 0.001: strong evidence relationship p < 0.01: Strong evidence relationship p < 0.05: Moderate evidence relationship p >= 0.05: significant relationship found Unlike chi-square test uses large-sample approximation, Fisher's exact test computes exact probability observing given table (extreme one) null hypothesis independence. tables larger 2x2, Fisher-Freeman-Halton extension used.

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: Use Fisher's exact test : expected cell frequency less 5 total sample size small (typically N < 30) 2x2 table (Fisher's exact standard ) want exact p-value rather approximation

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: large samples expected frequencies >= 5: Use chi_square() instead paired binary data (/): Use mcnemar_test() instead testing single proportion: Use binomial_test() instead

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: SPSS: CROSSTABS /STATISTICS=CHISQ (Fisher's exact test automatically reported 2x2 tables)

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: Fisher, R. . (1922). interpretation chi-square contingency tables, calculation P. Journal Royal Statistical Society, 85(1), 87-94. Freeman, G. H., & Halton, J. H. (1951). Note exact treatment contingency, goodness fit problems significance. Biometrika, 38(1/2), 141-149.

title: Count How Many People Chose Each Option — frequency

title: Count How Many People Chose Each Option — frequency
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... categorical variables want analyze. can list multiple variables separated commas, use helpers like starts_with("trust") weights Optional survey weights population-representative results. Without weights, get sample frequencies. weights, get population estimates. sort.frq order results: "none" (default): Keep original order "asc": Sort lowest highest frequency "desc": Sort highest lowest frequency show.na Include missing values table? (Default: TRUE) show.prc Show raw percentages including missing values? (Default: TRUE) show.valid Show percentages excluding missing values? (Default: TRUE) show.sum Show cumulative totals? (Default: TRUE) show.labels Show category labels available? (Default: "auto" - shows labels exist)

what: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: friedman_test() compares three related measurements subjects data normally distributed. non-parametric alternative repeated-measures ANOVA. Think : Comparing ratings multiple items respondents Testing whether scores change across three time points robust repeated-measures comparison works data shape test tells : Whether least one measurement significantly different others measurements tend rated higher lower (via mean ranks) strength overall effect (Kendall's W)

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
code: friedman_test(data, ..., weights = NULL, conf.level = 0.95)

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) wide format, one row per subject measurement separate column ... measurement variables compare (least 3). can list individually use helpers like starts_with("trust_") weights Optional survey weights population-representative results conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95 percent)

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: Test results showing whether measurements differ, including: Chi-Square statistic (Friedman test statistic) Degrees freedom (number measurements minus 1) P-value (measurements different?) Kendall's W (effect size: strong overall pattern?) Mean rank measurement (measurements higher/lower?)

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: P-value: p < 0.05, least one measurement significantly different p < 0.001: strong evidence differences p < 0.01: Strong evidence differences p < 0.05: Moderate evidence differences p > 0.05: significant differences found Kendall's W (Effect size: consistent pattern?): < 0.1: Negligible agreement/effect 0.1 - 0.3: Weak agreement 0.3 - 0.5: Moderate agreement 0.5 higher: Strong agreement Mean Ranks: Higher mean rank = measurement tends higher values Lower mean rank = measurement tends lower values Compare mean ranks see measurements stand

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: Use Friedman test : 3 related measurements subjects data normally distributed ordinal data (ratings, rankings) want robust alternative repeated-measures ANOVA comparing multiple ratings respondents

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: 2 related measurements: Use wilcoxon_test() instead normally distributed repeated measures: Use repeated-measures ANOVA independent groups: Use kruskal_wallis() instead

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: Friedman, M. (1937). use ranks avoid assumption normality implicit analysis variance. Journal American Statistical Association, 32(200), 675-701. Kendall, M. G., & Babington Smith, B. (1939). problem m rankings. Annals Mathematical Statistics, 10(3), 275-287.

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Compare three trust items (rated by same respondents) survey_data %>%   friedman_test(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #>  #> Friedman Test Results #> --------------------- #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - Number of conditions: 3 #>  #>   Ranks: #>   --------------------------- #>            Variable Mean Rank #>    trust_government      1.81 #>         trust_media      1.68 #>       trust_science      2.51 #>   --------------------------- #>  #>   Test Statistics: #>   ------------------------------------------- #>       N Chi-Square df p value Kendall's W sig #>    2135   1009.035  2       0       0.236 *** #>   ------------------------------------------- #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation (Kendall's W): #> - Weak agreement: 0.1 - 0.3 #> - Moderate agreement: 0.3 - 0.5 #> - Strong agreement: > 0.5  # Using tidyselect helpers survey_data %>%   friedman_test(starts_with("trust_")) #>  #> Friedman Test Results #> --------------------- #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - Number of conditions: 3 #>  #>   Ranks: #>   --------------------------- #>            Variable Mean Rank #>    trust_government      1.81 #>         trust_media      1.68 #>       trust_science      2.51 #>   --------------------------- #>  #>   Test Statistics: #>   ------------------------------------------- #>       N Chi-Square df p value Kendall's W sig #>    2135   1009.035  2       0       0.236 *** #>   ------------------------------------------- #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation (Kendall's W): #> - Weak agreement: 0.1 - 0.3 #> - Moderate agreement: 0.3 - 0.5 #> - Strong agreement: > 0.5  # Weighted analysis survey_data %>%   friedman_test(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,                 weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Friedman Test Results #> ------------------------------ #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - Number of conditions: 3 #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #>  #>   Ranks: #>   --------------------------- #>            Variable Mean Rank #>    trust_government      1.81 #>         trust_media      1.68 #>       trust_science      2.51 #>   --------------------------- #>  #>   Weighted Test Statistics: #>   ------------------------------------------- #>       N Chi-Square df p value Kendall's W sig #>    2150    846.423  2       0       0.197 *** #>   ------------------------------------------- #>  #> Note: Weighted analysis uses frequency-weighted ranks. #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation (Kendall's W): #> - Weak agreement: 0.1 - 0.3 #> - Moderate agreement: 0.3 - 0.5 #> - Strong agreement: > 0.5  # Grouped analysis (separate test per region) survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   friedman_test(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #>  #> Friedman Test Results #> --------------------- #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - Number of conditions: 3 #>  #>  #> Group: region = East #> -------------------- #>  #>   Ranks: #>   --------------------------- #>            Variable Mean Rank #>    trust_government      1.80 #>         trust_media      1.67 #>       trust_science      2.53 #>   --------------------------- #>  #>   Test Statistics: #>   ------------------------------------------ #>      N Chi-Square df p value Kendall's W sig #>    422      217.1  2       0       0.257 *** #>   ------------------------------------------ #>  #>  #> Group: region = West #> -------------------- #>  #>   Ranks: #>   --------------------------- #>            Variable Mean Rank #>    trust_government      1.81 #>         trust_media      1.68 #>       trust_science      2.50 #>   --------------------------- #>  #>   Test Statistics: #>   ------------------------------------------- #>       N Chi-Square df p value Kendall's W sig #>    1713    792.344  2       0       0.231 *** #>   ------------------------------------------- #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation (Kendall's W): #> - Weak agreement: 0.1 - 0.3 #> - Moderate agreement: 0.3 - 0.5 #> - Strong agreement: > 0.5

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
text: Calculates Kendall's tau-b rank correlation coefficients variables support weighted correlations, grouped data, multiple variable pairs. Provides significance testing SPSS-compatible output formatting. function computes tau-b, adjusted ties particularly suitable ordinal data assumptions Pearson correlation met. weighted correlations, uses survey-weighted rank calculations.

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
code: kendall_tau(   data,   ...,   weights = NULL,   alternative = c("two.sided", "less", "greater"),   use = c("pairwise", "listwise"),   na.rm = NULL )

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... variables want correlate. List two single correlation correlation matrix. can use helpers like starts_with("trust"). weights Optional survey weights population-representative results. alternative Direction test: "two.sided" (default): Two-tailed test "less": One-tailed test (negative correlation) "greater": One-tailed test (positive correlation) use handle missing values: "pairwise" (default): Pairwise deletion - correlation uses available cases "listwise": Listwise deletion - complete cases across variables na.rm Deprecated. Use use instead.

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
text: Correlation results showing rank-based relationships variables, including tau-b coefficient, p-value, z-score, sample size pair. multiple variables, correlation, significance, sample size matrices also provided.

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
text: Kendall's tau measures often pairs observations order (concordant) versus different order (discordant). particularly useful ordinal data, data outliers, small sample sizes, non-linear monotonic relationships. tau value ranges -1 +1: Strong positive (0.5 1.0): High values one variable tend go high values Moderate positive (0.3 0.5): tendency values increase together Weak positive (0 0.3): Slight tendency values increase together correlation (near 0): relationship variables Negative values: one variable increases, tends decrease output also provides: p-value: Probability seeing correlation chance (smaller = stronger evidence) n: Number observations used significance stars: Quick visual indicator statistical significance

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
text: Choose Kendall's tau : data ordinal (ranked categories) small sample size (< 30 observations) data outliers might affect Pearson correlation want conservative measure Spearman's rho

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
text: Kendall, M. G. (1938). new measure rank correlation. Biometrika, 30(1/2), 81–93. Kendall, M. G. (1945). treatment ties ranking problems. Biometrika, 33(3), 239–251. Agresti, . (2010). Analysis Ordinal Categorical Data (2nd ed.). John Wiley & Sons.

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
