what: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: kruskal_wallis() compares three groups data normally distributed ordinal data (like ratings rankings). non-parametric alternative one-way ANOVA. Think : extension Mann-Whitney test two groups robust way compare groups works data shape Perfect Likert scales, rankings, skewed distributions test tells : Whether least one group different others strong overall group effect (effect size) groups tend higher lower values (via mean ranks)

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
code: kruskal_wallis(data, ..., group, weights = NULL, conf.level = 0.95)

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... variables want compare groups. can list multiple variables use helpers like starts_with("satisfaction") group categorical variable defines groups (e.g., education, employment). Must least 2 groups (3+ meaningful use). weights Optional survey weights population-representative results conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95%)

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: Test results showing whether groups differ, including: H statistic (Kruskal-Wallis chi-square test statistic) Degrees freedom (number groups minus 1) P-value (groups different?) Effect size eta-squared (big group effect?) Mean rank group (groups higher/lower?)

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: P-value: p < 0.05, least one group significantly different p < 0.001: strong evidence group differences p < 0.01: Strong evidence group differences p < 0.05: Moderate evidence group differences p > 0.05: significant group differences found Effect Size Eta-squared (much groups matter?): < 0.01: Negligible effect 0.01-0.06: Small effect 0.06-0.14: Medium effect 0.14 higher: Large effect Mean Ranks: Higher mean rank = group tends higher values Lower mean rank = group tends lower values Compare mean ranks see pattern group differences

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: Use Kruskal-Wallis test : data normally distributed (skewed, outliers) ordinal data (rankings, Likert scales) Sample sizes small unequal across groups want robust alternative one-way ANOVA comparing satisfaction ratings, income, skewed variables

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: Kruskal-Wallis test significant: Look mean ranks see pattern Use pairwise Mann-Whitney tests Bonferroni correction find specific groups differ Consider effect sizes judge practical importance

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: 2 groups: Use mann_whitney() instead normally distributed data: Use oneway_anova() instead repeated measures (subjects): Use friedman_test() instead

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: Kruskal, W. H., & Wallis, W. . (1952). Use ranks one-criterion variance analysis. Journal American Statistical Association, 47(260), 583-621. Tomczak, M., & Tomczak, E. (2014). need report effect size estimates revisited. overview recommended measures effect size. Trends Sport Sciences, 1(21), 19-25.

what: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: levene_test() checks different groups similar amounts variation. important assumption many statistical tests - groups spread similar ways. test tells : Whether variance consistent across groups can trust standard ANOVA t-test results use alternative tests assume equal variance

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
code: levene_test(x, ...)  # Default S3 method levene_test(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' levene_test(x, ..., group, weights = NULL, center = c("mean", "median"))  # S3 method for class 'oneway_anova' levene_test(x, center = c("mean", "median"), ...)  # S3 method for class 't_test' levene_test(x, center = c("mean", "median"), ...)  # S3 method for class 'mann_whitney' levene_test(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'grouped_df' levene_test(x, variable, group = NULL, weights = NULL, center = "mean", ...)

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: x Either data test results t_test() oneway_anova() ... Variables test (using data frame) group grouping variable comparison weights Optional survey weights population-representative results center measure center: "mean" (default) "median" (robust) variable Variable test (using grouped data frame) data survey data (x test result)

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: Test results showing: Whether groups equal variances (p-value) F-statistic measuring variance differences variables meet assumption

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: P-value interpretation: p > 0.05: Good! Groups similar variance (assumption met) p ≤ 0.05: Problem - groups vary differently (assumption violated) Think like checking groups equally "spread ": Similar spread = can use standard tests Different spread = need special methods

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: Check variance equality : running t-tests ANOVA Comparing groups different sizes statistical test assumes equal variances see different standard deviations

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: Levene's test significant (p ≤ 0.05): t-tests: Use Welch's t-test (var.equal = FALSE) ANOVA: Use Welch's ANOVA Consider transforming data Use non-parametric alternatives Report equal variance assumption violated

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: can use function two ways: Standalone: Check variables equal variance tests: Pipe t_test() oneway_anova() verify assumptions

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: Always check assumption group comparisons Visual inspection (boxplots) can supplement test Large samples make test sensitive Use median-based test skewed data (center = "median") panic violated - alternatives exist!

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: Levene, H. (1960). Robust tests equality variances. . Olkin (Ed.), Contributions Probability Statistics (pp. 278–292). Stanford University Press. Brown, M. B., & Forsythe, . B. (1974). Robust tests equality variances. Journal American Statistical Association, 69(346), 364–367. IBM Corp. (2023). IBM SPSS Statistics 29 Algorithms. IBM Corporation.

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Standalone Levene test (test homogeneity of variances) survey_data %>% levene_test(life_satisfaction, group = region) #>  #> Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance  #> ------------------------------------------ #>  #> - Grouping variable: region #> - Center: mean #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Levene's Test Results: #> ------------------------------------------------------------------  #>           Variable F_statistic df1  df2 p_value sig      Conclusion #>  life_satisfaction       3.164   1 2419   0.075     Variances equal #> ------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - p > 0.05: Variances are homogeneous (equal variances assumed) #> - p <= 0.05: Variances are heterogeneous (equal variances NOT assumed)  # Multiple variables survey_data %>% levene_test(life_satisfaction, trust_government, group = region) #>  #> Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance  #> ------------------------------------------ #>  #> - Grouping variable: region #> - Center: mean #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Levene's Test Results: #> ------------------------------------------------------------------  #>           Variable F_statistic df1  df2 p_value sig      Conclusion #>  life_satisfaction       3.164   1 2419   0.075     Variances equal #> ------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> --- trust_government --- #>  #> Levene's Test Results: #> -----------------------------------------------------------------  #>          Variable F_statistic df1  df2 p_value sig      Conclusion #>  trust_government       0.145   1 2352   0.703     Variances equal #> -----------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - p > 0.05: Variances are homogeneous (equal variances assumed) #> - p <= 0.05: Variances are heterogeneous (equal variances NOT assumed)  # Weighted analysis survey_data %>% levene_test(income, group = education, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance  #> --------------------------------------------------- #>  #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #> - Center: mean #>  #>  #> --- income --- #>  #> Weighted Levene's Test Results: #> -----------------------------------------------------------  #>  Variable F_statistic df1  df2 p_value sig        Conclusion #>    income      102.05   3 2197       0 *** Variances unequal #> -----------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - p > 0.05: Variances are homogeneous (equal variances assumed) #> - p <= 0.05: Variances are heterogeneous (equal variances NOT assumed)  # Piped after ANOVA (common workflow) result <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education) result %>% levene_test() #>  #> Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance  #> ------------------------------------------ #>  #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Center: mean #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Levene's Test Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------  #>           Variable F_statistic df1  df2 p_value sig        Conclusion #>  life_satisfaction      31.634   3 2417       0 *** Variances unequal #> --------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - p > 0.05: Variances are homogeneous (equal variances assumed) #> - p <= 0.05: Variances are heterogeneous (equal variances NOT assumed) #>  #> Recommendation based on Levene test: #> - Use Welch's t-test (unequal variances)  # Piped after t-test survey_data %>%   t_test(age, group = gender) %>%   levene_test() #>  #> Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance  #> ------------------------------------------ #>  #> - Grouping variable: gender #> - Center: mean #>  #>  #> --- age --- #>  #> Levene's Test Results: #> ---------------------------------------------------------  #>  Variable F_statistic df1  df2 p_value sig      Conclusion #>       age       0.534   1 2498   0.465     Variances equal #> ---------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - p > 0.05: Variances are homogeneous (equal variances assumed) #> - p <= 0.05: Variances are heterogeneous (equal variances NOT assumed) #>  #> Recommendation based on Levene test: #> - Student's t-test or Welch's t-test both appropriate  # Using mean instead of median as center survey_data %>% levene_test(income, group = region, center = "mean") #>  #> Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance  #> ------------------------------------------ #>  #> - Grouping variable: region #> - Center: mean #>  #>  #> --- income --- #>  #> Levene's Test Results: #> ---------------------------------------------------------  #>  Variable F_statistic df1  df2 p_value sig      Conclusion #>    income       1.631   1 2184   0.202     Variances equal #> ---------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - p > 0.05: Variances are homogeneous (equal variances assumed) #> - p <= 0.05: Variances are heterogeneous (equal variances NOT assumed)

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
text: linear_regression() performs bivariate multiple linear regression SPSS-compatible output. Wraps stats::lm() adds standardized coefficients (Beta), formatted ANOVA table, model summary matching SPSS REGRESSION output. Supports two interface styles: Formula interface: linear_regression(data, life_satisfaction ~ age + education) SPSS-style: linear_regression(data, dependent = life_satisfaction, predictors = c(age, education))

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
code: linear_regression(   data,   formula = NULL,   dependent = NULL,   predictors = NULL,   weights = NULL,   use = c("listwise", "pairwise"),   standardized = TRUE,   conf.level = 0.95 )

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
text: data survey data (data frame tibble). grouped (via dplyr::group_by()), separate regressions run group. formula formula specifying model (e.g., y ~ x1 + x2). provided, dependent predictors ignored. dependent dependent variable (unquoted). Used predictors formula given. predictors Predictor variable(s) (unquoted, supports tidyselect). Used dependent formula given. weights Optional survey weights (unquoted variable name). specified, weighted least squares (WLS) used, matching SPSS WEIGHT . use handle missing data: "listwise" (default) drops case missing value variable (matching SPSS /MISSING LISTWISE). "pairwise" computes regression pairwise covariance/correlation matrix, retaining cases (matching SPSS /MISSING PAIRWISE). standardized Logical. TRUE (default), standardized coefficients (Beta) calculated included output. conf.level Confidence level coefficient intervals (default 0.95).

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
text: object class "linear_regression" containing: coefficients Tibble B, Std.Error, Beta, t, p, CI_lower, CI_upper model_summary List R, R_squared, adj_R_squared, std_error anova Tibble Sum Squares, df, Mean Square, F, Sig. descriptives Tibble Mean, Std.Deviation, N variables model underlying lm object formula formula used n Sample size (listwise complete cases) dependent Name dependent variable predictor_names Names predictor variables weighted Logical indicating whether weights used weight_name Name weight variable (NULL)

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
text: output includes four sections matching SPSS REGRESSION output: Model Summary: R, R-squared, Adjusted R-squared, Standard Error Estimate. R-squared tells much variance dependent variable explained predictors. ANOVA: Tests whether overall model significant. significant F-test means least one predictor matters. Coefficients: B (unstandardized), Beta (standardized), t-value, p-value, confidence intervals predictor. Descriptives: Mean, SD, N variables model. Interpreting coefficients: B (unstandardized): 1-unit increase predictor, dependent variable changes B units Beta (standardized): Allows comparison across predictors different scales. Larger absolute Beta = stronger effect p-value: Values 0.05 indicate statistically significant predictors

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
text: Use linear_regression() : dependent variable continuous (e.g., income, satisfaction score) want predict outcome one predictors need standardized coefficients compare predictor importance binary outcomes (yes/, 0/1), use logistic_regression instead.

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
text: Missing Data: default, listwise deletion used (matching SPSS REGRESSION /MISSING LISTWISE). Set use = "pairwise" match SPSS /MISSING PAIRWISE, computes regression pairwise covariance matrix. Pairwise deletion retains cases produces results closer SPSS output data varying patterns missingness. Weights: weights specified, treated frequency weights (matching SPSS WEIGHT behavior). model fitted using weighted least squares via lm(weights = ...). Standardized Coefficients: Beta = B * (SD_x / SD_y). matches SPSS standardized coefficient output. available intercept. Grouped Analysis: data grouped via dplyr::group_by(), separate regression run group (matching SPSS SPLIT FILE ).

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
text: logistic_regression() performs binary logistic regression SPSS-compatible output. Wraps stats::glm(family = binomial) adds odds ratios, pseudo R-squared measures, classification table, model tests matching SPSS LOGISTIC REGRESSION output. Supports two interface styles: Formula interface: logistic_regression(data, high_satisfaction ~ age + income) SPSS-style: logistic_regression(data, dependent = high_satisfaction, predictors = c(age, income))

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
code: logistic_regression(   data,   formula = NULL,   dependent = NULL,   predictors = NULL,   weights = NULL,   conf.level = 0.95 )

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
text: data survey data (data frame tibble). grouped (via dplyr::group_by()), separate regressions run group. formula formula specifying model (e.g., y ~ x1 + x2). provided, dependent predictors ignored. dependent dependent variable (unquoted). Used predictors formula given. Must binary (0/1 two-level factor). predictors Predictor variable(s) (unquoted, supports tidyselect). Used dependent formula given. weights Optional survey weights (unquoted variable name). specified, weighted maximum likelihood estimation used, matching SPSS WEIGHT behavior. conf.level Confidence level odds ratio intervals (default 0.95).

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
text: object class "logistic_regression" containing: coefficients Tibble B, S.E., Wald, df, Sig., Exp(B), CI_lower, CI_upper model_summary List minus2LL, cox_snell_r2, nagelkerke_r2, mcfadden_r2 omnibus_test List chi_sq, df, p overall model test classification List table, overall_pct, pct_correct_0, pct_correct_1 hosmer_lemeshow List chi_sq, df, p (goodness--fit test) model underlying glm object formula formula used n Sample size (listwise complete cases) dependent Name dependent variable predictor_names Names predictor variables weighted Logical indicating whether weights used weight_name Name weight variable (NULL)

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
text: output includes five sections matching SPSS LOGISTIC REGRESSION output: Omnibus Test: Tests whether model whole significant. significant chi-square means model predicts better chance. Model Summary: -2 Log Likelihood pseudo R-squared values. Lower -2LL = better fit. Higher R-squared = variance explained. Hosmer-Lemeshow Test: Goodness--fit test. non-significant result (p > 0.05) means model fits data well. Classification Table: well model classifies cases. Shows percentage correctly predicted group overall. Coefficients: B, Wald test, odds ratios (Exp(B)), CIs. Interpreting odds ratios (Exp(B)): Exp(B) > 1: Predictor increases odds outcome Exp(B) < 1: Predictor decreases odds outcome Exp(B) = 1: Predictor effect odds

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
text: Use logistic_regression() : dependent variable binary (yes/, 0/1, pass/fail) want predict group membership one predictors need odds ratios interpret predictor effects continuous outcomes, use linear_regression instead.

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
text: Dependent Variable: Must binary. Factors exactly 2 levels automatically converted 0/1 (first level = 0, second level = 1). Numeric variables must contain 0 1 values. Missing Data: Listwise deletion used (matching SPSS LOGISTIC REGRESSION default behavior). Weights: weights specified, treated frequency weights (matching SPSS WEIGHT behavior). Pseudo R-squared: Three measures reported: Cox & Snell R-squared (bounded 1) Nagelkerke R-squared (adjusted reach 1) McFadden R-squared (1 - LL_model/LL_null) Grouped Analysis: data grouped via dplyr::group_by(), separate regression run group (matching SPSS SPLIT FILE ).

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression

what: Compare Two Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — mann_whitney
title: Compare Two Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — mann_whitney
text: mann_whitney() compares two groups data normally distributed ordinal data (like ratings rankings). go-alternative t-tests appropriate. Think : robust way compare groups works data shape Perfect Likert scales, ratings, skewed distributions test compares typical values groups test tells : Whether one group tends higher values strong difference (effect size) group higher average ranks

title: Compare Two Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — mann_whitney
code: mann_whitney(   data,   ...,   group,   weights = NULL,   mu = 0,   alternative = c("two.sided", "less", "greater"),   conf.level = 0.95 )

title: Compare Two Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — mann_whitney
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... variables want compare groups. can list multiple variables use helpers like starts_with("satisfaction") group categorical variable defines two groups (e.g., gender, treatment/control). Must exactly two groups. weights Optional survey weights population-representative results mu hypothesized difference (Default: 0, meaning difference) alternative Direction test: "two.sided" (default): Test groups different "greater": Test group 1 > group 2 "less": Test group 1 < group 2 conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95%)

title: Compare Two Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — mann_whitney
text: Test results showing whether groups differ, including: U W statistics (test statistics) Z-score p-value (groups different?) Effect size r (big difference?) Rank means group (group higher?)

title: Compare Two Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — mann_whitney
text: P-value: p < 0.05, groups significantly different p < 0.001: strong evidence difference p < 0.01: Strong evidence difference p < 0.05: Moderate evidence difference p ≥ 0.05: significant difference found Effect Size r (big difference?): |r| < 0.1: Negligible difference |r| ~ 0.1: Small difference |r| ~ 0.3: Medium difference |r| ~ 0.5: Large difference |r| > 0.5: large difference Rank Mean Difference: Positive: Group 1 tends higher values Negative: Group 2 tends higher values Zero: Groups similar distributions

title: Compare Two Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — mann_whitney
text: Use Mann-Whitney test : data normally distributed (skewed, outliers) ordinal data (rankings, Likert scales) Sample sizes small (< 30 per group) want robust alternative t-test comparing satisfaction ratings, income, skewed variables

title: Compare Two Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — mann_whitney
text: Works data distribution affected outliers Valid ordinal data assumptions variance robust real-world data

title: Compare Two Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — mann_whitney
text: group least 5 observations test compares distributions, just means Look p-values effect sizes Consider plotting data see pattern Use Likert scales rating data

title: Compare Two Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — mann_whitney
text: Mann, H. B., & Whitney, D. R. (1947). test whether one two random variables stochastically larger . Annals Mathematical Statistics, 18(1), 50-60. Wilcoxon, F. (1945). Individual comparisons ranking methods. Biometrics Bulletin, 1(6), 80-83. Lumley, T., & Scott, . (2013). Two-sample rank tests complex sampling. Biometrika, 100(4), 831-842.

what: mariposa: Marburg Initiative for Political and Social Analysis - Statistical Tools for Survey Data — mariposa-package
title: mariposa: Marburg Initiative for Political and Social Analysis - Statistical Tools for Survey Data — mariposa-package
text: Professional statistical analysis survey data full support survey weights, grouped operations, tidyverse integration. Provides 44 functions descriptive statistics, hypothesis testing, correlation analysis, post-hoc comparisons, weighted statistics, scale analysis, regression, non-parametric tests, exact tests, factorial ANOVA, ANCOVA. results validated SPSS v29 reproducibility. Designed survey researchers, social scientists, students working complex survey designs.

title: mariposa: Marburg Initiative for Political and Social Analysis - Statistical Tools for Survey Data — mariposa-package
text: Maintainer: Yannick Diehl yannick.diehl@gmail.com

what: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test
title: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test
text: mcnemar_test() tests whether paired proportions changed two dichotomous measurements. Use /comparisons categorical outcomes. Think : paired comparison test binary (yes/) data categorical equivalent paired t-test Tests whether proportion "changers" symmetric test tells : Whether paired proportions changed significantly asymptotic exact p-values maximum reliability number discordant pairs (actually changed)

title: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test
code: mcnemar_test(data, var1, var2, weights = NULL, correct = TRUE, ...)

title: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) var1 First dichotomous variable (0/1 two-level factor) var2 Second dichotomous variable (0/1 two-level factor) weights Optional survey weights population-representative results correct Logical, whether apply continuity correction (default: TRUE) ... Additional arguments (currently unused)

title: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test
text: Test results showing whether paired proportions changed, including: McNemar chi-square statistic (continuity correction) Asymptotic p-value Exact binomial p-value (two-sided) 2x2 contingency table Discordant pair counts (b c)

title: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test
text: P-value: p < 0.05, paired proportions significantly different p < 0.001: strong evidence change p < 0.01: Strong evidence change p < 0.05: Moderate evidence change p >= 0.05: significant change found Exact vs Asymptotic: exact binomial p-value reliable small samples. large samples, p-values similar. Discordant Pairs: pairs two measurements differ (b c) contribute test. b approximately equals c, evidence systematic change.

title: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test
text: Use McNemar's test : paired matched binary data comparing /proportions variables must dichotomous (exactly 2 levels)

title: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test
text: 2x2 table discordant cells b c: $$\chi^2 = \frac{(|b - c| - 1)^2}{b + c}$$ (continuity correction) exact test uses binomial test discordant pairs.

title: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test
text: unpaired categorical data: Use chi_square() instead paired ordinal/continuous data: Use wilcoxon_test() instead paired data 2 levels: Consider Bowker test symmetry

title: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test
text: SPSS: CROSSTABS /STATISTICS=MCNEMAR

title: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test
text: McNemar, Q. (1947). Note sampling error difference correlated proportions percentages. Psychometrika, 12(2), 153-157.

title: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Create dichotomous variables test_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(     trust_gov_high = as.integer(trust_government >= 4),     trust_media_high = as.integer(trust_media >= 4)   )  # McNemar test test_data %>%   mcnemar_test(var1 = trust_gov_high, var2 = trust_media_high) #> McNemar Test Results #> -------------------- #>  #> - Variable 1: trust_gov_high #> - Variable 2: trust_media_high #>  #> 2x2 Contingency Table: #> ----------------------------------------  #>    v2 #> v1     0    1 #>   0 1342  338 #>   1  448   99 #> ----------------------------------------  #>  #> Test Results: #> -----------------------------------------  #>  Chi-Sq (cc) p (asymp) p (exact)    N Sig  #>       15.116     <.001     <.001 2227 ***  #> -----------------------------------------  #>  #> Discordant pairs: b = 338, c = 448 #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05  # Grouped analysis test_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   mcnemar_test(var1 = trust_gov_high, var2 = trust_media_high) #> McNemar Test Results #> -------------------- #>  #> - Variable 1: trust_gov_high #> - Variable 2: trust_media_high #>  #>  #> Group: region = East #> -------------------- #> -----------------------------------  #>  Chi-Sq p (asymp) p (exact)   N Sig  #>   8.255    0.0041    0.0039 435  **  #> -----------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Group: region = West #> -------------------- #> ------------------------------------  #>  Chi-Sq p (asymp) p (exact)    N Sig  #>   8.242    0.0041    0.0041 1792  **  #> ------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

what: Compare Multiple Groups: Are Their Averages Different? — oneway_anova
title: Compare Multiple Groups: Are Their Averages Different? — oneway_anova
text: oneway_anova() helps determine average values differ across three groups. example, average income vary education level? customer satisfaction different across regions? Think : extension t-test two groups way test group membership affects outcomes tool identify meaningful group differences test tells : Whether least one group different others much variation explained group membership variance assumption fits data best

title: Compare Multiple Groups: Are Their Averages Different? — oneway_anova
code: oneway_anova(   data,   ...,   group,   weights = NULL,   var.equal = TRUE,   conf.level = 0.95 )

title: Compare Multiple Groups: Are Their Averages Different? — oneway_anova
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables want analyze. can list multiple variables use helpers like starts_with("income") group categorical variable defines groups (e.g., education, region, age_group). Must least 3 groups ANOVA. weights Optional survey weights population-representative results var.equal assume groups similar variance? (Default: TRUE) TRUE: Standard ANOVA (assumes equal variances) FALSE: Welch's ANOVA (allows unequal variances) conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95%)

title: Compare Multiple Groups: Are Their Averages Different? — oneway_anova
text: ANOVA results showing whether groups differ, including: F-statistic p-value (groups different?) Effect sizes (much groups matter?) Group statistics (means standard deviations) standard Welch ANOVA results

title: Compare Multiple Groups: Are Their Averages Different? — oneway_anova
text: P-value: p < 0.05, least one group average different p < 0.001: strong evidence group differences p < 0.01: Strong evidence group differences p < 0.05: Moderate evidence group differences p ≥ 0.05: significant group differences found Effect Sizes (much groups matter?): Eta-squared: Proportion variance explained groups < 0.01: Negligible effect 0.01-0.06: Small effect 0.06-0.14: Medium effect 0.14 higher: Large effect Omega-squared: conservative estimate (usually preferred)

title: Compare Multiple Groups: Are Their Averages Different? — oneway_anova
text: Use ANOVA : one numeric outcome (income, satisfaction score, etc.) one categorical grouping variable 3+ groups want know group averages differ data roughly normally distributed within groups

title: Compare Multiple Groups: Are Their Averages Different? — oneway_anova
text: Standard ANOVA (var.equal = TRUE): Use group variances similar Welch's ANOVA (var.equal = FALSE): Use group variances differ unsure function shows results comparison

title: Compare Multiple Groups: Are Their Averages Different? — oneway_anova
text: ANOVA significant: Look group means see pattern Use tukey_test() find specific groups differ Consider effect sizes judge practical importance

title: Compare Multiple Groups: Are Their Averages Different? — oneway_anova
text: Check group sufficient observations (ideally 20+) Look p-values effect sizes Use Welch's ANOVA group sizes unequal Follow post-hoc tests identify specific differences Welch's robust test equality means

title: Compare Multiple Groups: Are Their Averages Different? — oneway_anova

Biometrika, 38(3/4), 330-336. Olejnik, S., & Algina, J. (2003). Generalized eta omega squared statistics: measures effect size common research designs. Psychological Methods, 8(4), 434-447.

homogeneous (equal variances assumed) #> - p <= 0.05: Variances are heterogeneous (equal variances NOT assumed) #>  #> Recommendation based on Levene test: #> - Use Welch's t-test (unequal variances) what: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: pairwise_wilcoxon() tells exactly pairs measurements differ Friedman test finds overall differences. performs pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank tests p-value correction. Think : Friedman says "differences somewhere among measurements" Pairwise Wilcoxon says "specifically, Measurement differs Measurement C" way make possible pairwise comparisons repeated measures

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
code: pairwise_wilcoxon(x, ...)  # Default S3 method pairwise_wilcoxon(x, ...)

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: x Friedman test results friedman_test() ... Additional arguments passed methods. method friedman_test objects accepts p_adjust (character): method adjusting p-values multiple comparisons. Options: "bonferroni" (default, conservative), "holm", "BH", "hochberg", "hommel", "", "fdr", "none".

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: Pairwise comparison results showing: measurement pairs significantly different Z-statistics Wilcoxon signed-rank tests Adjusted p-values (controlling multiple comparisons)

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: Z-Statistics: Based Wilcoxon signed-rank test pair Large absolute Z values indicate big differences two measurements Positive Z: Values var1 tend higher var2 Negative Z: Values var2 tend higher var1 Adjusted P-values: Control multiple comparisons p < 0.05: Measurements significantly different p >= 0.05: significant difference measurements

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: pair measurements, Wilcoxon signed-rank test: Computes differences two measurements Ranks absolute differences Computes Z-statistic based rank sums Uses normal approximation tie correction

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: Bonferroni (default): conservative, multiplies p number comparisons Holm: Step-method, less conservative Bonferroni BH: Controls false discovery rate, good many comparisons

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: Use pairwise Wilcoxon : Friedman test shows significant differences (p < 0.05) want know specific measurements differ data ordinal violate normality assumptions repeated measures matched groups

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: Non-parametric post-hoc repeated measures (like Dunn independent groups) Follow-friedman_test(), like dunn_test() follows kruskal_wallis() pairwise comparison uses wilcoxon_test() logic internally

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: Wilcoxon, F. (1945). Individual comparisons ranking methods. Biometrics Bulletin, 1(6), 80-83.

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Perform Friedman followed by pairwise Wilcoxon post-hoc friedman_result <- survey_data %>%   friedman_test(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science)  # Pairwise Wilcoxon comparisons (default: Bonferroni) friedman_result %>% pairwise_wilcoxon() #> Pairwise Wilcoxon Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) Results #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - P-value adjustment: Bonferroni #> - Number of comparisons: 3 #>  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>             Var 1         Var 2      Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>  trust_government   trust_media -5.097     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  trust_government trust_science 25.945     <.001   <.001 ***  #>       trust_media trust_science 29.091     <.001   <.001 ***  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive Z: First variable tends to have higher values #> - Negative Z: Second variable tends to have higher values #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons  # With Holm correction (less conservative) friedman_result %>% pairwise_wilcoxon(p_adjust = "holm") #> Pairwise Wilcoxon Post-Hoc Test (Holm) Results #> ---------------------------------------------- #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - P-value adjustment: Holm #> - Number of comparisons: 3 #>  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>             Var 1         Var 2      Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>  trust_government   trust_media -5.097     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  trust_government trust_science 25.945     <.001   <.001 ***  #>       trust_media trust_science 29.091     <.001   <.001 ***  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive Z: First variable tends to have higher values #> - Negative Z: Second variable tends to have higher values #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons  # With Benjamini-Hochberg (controls false discovery rate) friedman_result %>% pairwise_wilcoxon(p_adjust = "BH") #> Pairwise Wilcoxon Post-Hoc Test (Benjamini-Hochberg) Results #> ------------------------------------------------------------ #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - P-value adjustment: Benjamini-Hochberg #> - Number of comparisons: 3 #>  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>             Var 1         Var 2      Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>  trust_government   trust_media -5.097     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  trust_government trust_science 25.945     <.001   <.001 ***  #>       trust_media trust_science 29.091     <.001   <.001 ***  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive Z: First variable tends to have higher values #> - Negative Z: Second variable tends to have higher values #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons  # With weights fw_weighted <- survey_data %>%   friedman_test(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,                 weights = sampling_weight)  fw_weighted %>% pairwise_wilcoxon() #> Weighted Pairwise Wilcoxon Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) Results #> ------------------------------------------------------------- #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #> - P-value adjustment: Bonferroni #> - Number of comparisons: 3 #>  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>             Var 1         Var 2      Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>  trust_government   trust_media -5.033     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  trust_government trust_science 25.997     <.001   <.001 ***  #>       trust_media trust_science 29.095     <.001   <.001 ***  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive Z: First variable tends to have higher values #> - Negative Z: Second variable tends to have higher values #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons  # Grouped analysis fw_grouped <- survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   friedman_test(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science)  fw_grouped %>% pairwise_wilcoxon() #> Pairwise Wilcoxon Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) Results #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - P-value adjustment: Bonferroni #> - Number of comparisons: 3 #>  #>  #> Group: region = East #> -------------------- #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>             Var 1         Var 2      Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>  trust_government   trust_media -2.727     0.006   0.019   *  #>  trust_government trust_science 11.635     <.001   <.001 ***  #>       trust_media trust_science 13.820     <.001   <.001 ***  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Group: region = West #> -------------------- #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>             Var 1         Var 2      Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>  trust_government   trust_media -4.346     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  trust_government trust_science 23.191     <.001   <.001 ***  #>       trust_media trust_science 25.630     <.001   <.001 ***  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive Z: First variable tends to have higher values #> - Negative Z: Second variable tends to have higher values #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons

what: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: pearson_cor() shows strongly numeric variables related . example, age related income? satisfaction increase experience? helps understand patterns data. correlation tells : Direction: Positive (increase together) negative (one increases decreases) Strength: closely variables move together (0 = relationship 1 = perfect relationship) Significance: Whether relationship real due chance

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
code: pearson_cor(   data,   ...,   weights = NULL,   conf.level = 0.95,   alternative = c("two.sided", "less", "greater"),   use = c("pairwise", "listwise"),   na.rm = NULL )

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables want correlate. List two single correlation correlation matrix. weights Optional survey weights population-representative results conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95%) alternative Direction test: "two.sided" (default), "less", "greater". use handle missing values: "pairwise" (default): Use available data pair "listwise": use complete cases across variables na.rm Deprecated. Use use instead.

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: Correlation results showing relationships variables, including: Correlation coefficient (r): Strength direction relationship P-value: Whether relationship statistically significant Confidence interval: Range plausible correlation values Sample size: Number observations used

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: Correlation coefficient (r) ranges -1 +1: +1: Perfect positive relationship (one goes , always goes ) 0: linear relationship -1: Perfect negative relationship (one goes , always goes ) Interpreting strength (absolute value r): 0.00 - 0.10: Negligible relationship 0.10 - 0.30: Weak relationship 0.30 - 0.50: Moderate relationship 0.50 - 0.70: Strong relationship 0.70 - 0.90: strong relationship 0.90 - 1.00: Extremely strong relationship P-value interpretation: p < 0.001: strong evidence relationship p < 0.01: Strong evidence relationship p < 0.05: Moderate evidence relationship p >= 0.05: significant relationship found correlation 0.65 p < 0.001 means: Strong positive relationship (r = 0.65) one variable increases, tends increase unlikely due chance (p < 0.001) 42% variation shared (r-squared = 0.65 squared = 0.42)

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: Use Pearson correlation : variables numeric continuous expect linear relationship Data roughly normally distributed want measure strength linear association use : Data extreme outliers (consider Spearman instead) Relationship curved/non-linear Variables categorical (use chi-squared test) need establish causation (correlation imply causation)

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: Always plot data first check non-linear patterns Consider statistical significance (p-value) practical importance (r value) Remember: correlation imply causation Check outliers might inflate deflate correlations Use Spearman correlation ordinal data non-normal distributions

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical Power Analysis Behavioral Sciences (2nd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum Associates. Fisher, R. . (1915). Frequency distribution values correlation coefficient samples indefinitely large population. Biometrika, 10(4), 507–521.

what: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
text: pomps() transforms scores 0-100 scale using Percent Maximum Possible Scores method. makes different scales directly comparable regardless original range. R equivalent SPSS formula: COMPUTE v81p = ((v81 - 1) / (7 - 1)) * 100. score 0 means minimum possible score, 100 means maximum. transformation preserves correlations variables.

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
code: pomps(x, scale_min = NULL, scale_max = NULL)

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
text: x numeric vector transform (e.g., column data). scale_min theoretical minimum scale. NULL (default), observed minimum x used. scale_max theoretical maximum scale. NULL (default), observed maximum x used.

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
text: numeric vector length x, values rescaled 0-100 range.

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
text: POMPS = ((score - scale_min) / (scale_max - scale_min)) * 100

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
text: default, pomps() uses observed minimum maximum data. However, Likert scales specify theoretical range: 1-5 Likert scale: scale_min = 1, scale_max = 5 1-7 Likert scale: scale_min = 1, scale_max = 7 0-10 scale: scale_min = 0, scale_max = 10 Using theoretical values ensures transformation consistent across samples time points.

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
text: Comparing variables measured different scales Creating profile plots across scales different ranges Reporting scale scores intuitive 0-100 format

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Transform a 1-5 Likert scale to POMPS survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(trust_gov_pomps = pomps(trust_government, scale_min = 1, scale_max = 5))  # Transform multiple variables with the same scale survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(across(     c(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science),     ~ pomps(.x, scale_min = 1, scale_max = 5),     .names = "{.col}_pomps"   ))  # Auto-detect range (uses observed min/max) survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(age_pomps = pomps(age))

what: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
text: reliability() calculates Cronbach's Alpha detailed item statistics evaluate whether survey items form reliable scale. R equivalent SPSS's RELIABILITY /MODEL=ALPHA /STATISTICS=DESCRIPTIVE CORR /SUMMARY=TOTAL. example, 3 items measuring "trust", reliability analysis tells whether items consistently measure concept.

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
code: reliability(data, ..., weights = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... items analyze. Use bare column names separated commas, tidyselect helpers like starts_with("trust"). weights Optional survey weights population-representative results na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE). Uses listwise deletion (complete cases across items).

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
text: reliability result object containing: alpha Cronbach's Alpha (unstandardized) alpha_standardized Cronbach's Alpha based standardized items n_items Number items scale item_statistics Mean, SD, N item item_total Corrected Item-Total Correlation Alpha Item Deleted inter_item_cor Inter-item correlation matrix n Sample size (listwise)

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
text: Cronbach's Alpha tells internally consistent scale : Alpha > 0.90: Excellent reliability Alpha 0.80 - 0.90: Good reliability Alpha 0.70 - 0.80: Acceptable reliability Alpha 0.60 - 0.70: Questionable reliability Alpha < 0.60: Poor reliability - reconsider items Item-Total Correlation shows well item fits scale: Values > 0.40: Item fits well Values 0.20 - 0.40: Item may need review Values < 0.20: Consider removing item Alpha Item Deleted shows happens remove item: alpha increases removing item, item hurts reliability alpha decreases, item contributes scale

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
text: Run reliability() creating scale scores scale_index: Select items Check reliability acceptable (alpha > .70), create index , review items consider removing problematic ones

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability

Items: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - N of Items: 3 #>  #> Reliability Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>   Cronbach's Alpha:              0.047 #>   Alpha (standardized):          0.048 #>   N of Items:                    3 #>   N (listwise):                  2135 #>  #> Item Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item  mean    sd    n #>  trust_government 2.621 1.162 2135 #>       trust_media 2.430 1.156 2135 #>     trust_science 3.624 1.034 2135 #>  #> Inter-Item Correlation Matrix: #> ------------------------------  #>                  trust_government trust_media trust_science #> trust_government            1.000       0.014         0.020 #> trust_media                 0.014       1.000         0.015 #> trust_science               0.020       0.015         1.000 #> ------------------------------  #>  #> Item-Total Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item scale_mean_deleted scale_var_deleted corrected_r #>  trust_government               6.05             2.440       0.024 #>       trust_media               6.25             2.467       0.020 #>     trust_science               5.05             2.723       0.025 #>  alpha_deleted #>          0.029 #>          0.040 #>          0.027  # With survey weights reliability(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,             weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Reliability Analysis Results #> ------------------------------------- #> - Items: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - N of Items: 3 #> - Weights: sampling_weight #>  #> Reliability Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>   Cronbach's Alpha:              0.052 #>   Alpha (standardized):          0.053 #>   N of Items:                    3 #>   N (listwise):                  2150.20 (weighted) #>  #> Item Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item  mean    sd      n #>  trust_government 2.621 1.162 2150.2 #>       trust_media 2.434 1.158 2150.2 #>     trust_science 3.624 1.033 2150.2 #>  #> Inter-Item Correlation Matrix: #> ------------------------------  #>                  trust_government trust_media trust_science #> trust_government            1.000       0.017         0.021 #> trust_media                 0.017       1.000         0.017 #> trust_science               0.021       0.017         1.000 #> ------------------------------  #>  #> Item-Total Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item scale_mean_deleted scale_var_deleted corrected_r #>  trust_government               6.06             2.451       0.026 #>       trust_media               6.25             2.469       0.023 #>     trust_science               5.05             2.737       0.027 #>  alpha_deleted #>          0.033 #>          0.041 #>          0.033  # Using tidyselect helpers reliability(survey_data, starts_with("trust")) #>  #> Reliability Analysis Results #> ---------------------------- #> - Items: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - N of Items: 3 #>  #> Reliability Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>   Cronbach's Alpha:              0.047 #>   Alpha (standardized):          0.048 #>   N of Items:                    3 #>   N (listwise):                  2135 #>  #> Item Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item  mean    sd    n #>  trust_government 2.621 1.162 2135 #>       trust_media 2.430 1.156 2135 #>     trust_science 3.624 1.034 2135 #>  #> Inter-Item Correlation Matrix: #> ------------------------------  #>                  trust_government trust_media trust_science #> trust_government            1.000       0.014         0.020 #> trust_media                 0.014       1.000         0.015 #> trust_science               0.020       0.015         1.000 #> ------------------------------  #>  #> Item-Total Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item scale_mean_deleted scale_var_deleted corrected_r #>  trust_government               6.05             2.440       0.024 #>       trust_media               6.25             2.467       0.020 #>     trust_science               5.05             2.723       0.025 #>  alpha_deleted #>          0.029 #>          0.040 #>          0.027  # Grouped by region survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   reliability(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #>  #> Reliability Analysis Results #> ---------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = 1 #> ----------------- #> - what: Create a Mean Index Across Items — scale_index
title: Create a Mean Index Across Items — scale_index
text: scale_index() calculates mean across multiple items respondent - standard way create scale scores survey items. R equivalent SPSS's COMPUTE m_X = MEAN(var1, var2, var3). example, measured trust 3 items (trust government, trust media, trust science), scale_index() creates single trust score averaging across items.

title: Create a Mean Index Across Items — scale_index
code: scale_index(data, ..., min_valid = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Create a Mean Index Across Items — scale_index
text: data survey data (data frame tibble). used inside mutate(), pass . use pick(). ... items average. Use bare column names separated commas, tidyselect helpers like starts_with("trust"). variables specified, numeric columns data used (useful pick()). min_valid Minimum number non-missing items required compute mean. respondent fewer valid items, NA returned row. works like SPSS's MEAN.x() syntax: min_valid = 2 corresponds MEAN.2(, b, c) SPSS. Default NULL (compute mean least 1 value valid). na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE)

title: Create a Mean Index Across Items — scale_index
text: numeric vector one value per row - mean across selected items. Use inside dplyr::mutate() add new column data.

title: Create a Mean Index Across Items — scale_index
text: respondent (row), scale_index() computes arithmetic mean selected items. Missing values ignored default, respondent answered 2 3 items still gets score.

title: Create a Mean Index Across Items — scale_index
text: practice, often want require minimum number valid responses. someone answered 1 5 items, mean based single item may reliable. Set min_valid control : min_valid = NULL (default): Compute mean number valid values (least 1) min_valid = 2: Require least 2 valid values min_valid = 3: Require least 3 valid values

title: Create a Mean Index Across Items — scale_index
text: Use scale_index() checking reliability reliability: Run reliability() check items form reliable scale Cronbach's Alpha acceptable (typically > .70), create index Use index analyses (t-tests, correlations, regression)

title: Create a Mean Index Across Items — scale_index
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Create a trust scale from 3 items survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust = scale_index(., trust_government, trust_media, trust_science))  # Using pick() - works with both %>% and |> pipes survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust = scale_index(     pick(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science)   ))  # Using tidyselect helpers survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust = scale_index(., starts_with("trust")))  # Require at least 2 valid items (like SPSS MEAN.2) survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust = scale_index(., trust_government, trust_media,                                trust_science, min_valid = 2))  # Standalone usage (returns vector) scores <- scale_index(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science)

what: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: scheffe_test() tells groups differ ANOVA, using conservative approach. like Tukey's test even careful avoiding false positives. Think : cautious post-hoc test available way compare groups sample sizes unequal Insurance finding differences real

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
code: scheffe_test(x, conf.level = 0.95, ...)  # Default S3 method scheffe_test(x, conf.level = 0.95, ...)

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: x ANOVA results oneway_anova() conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95%) ... Additional arguments (currently unused)

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Pairwise comparison results showing: group pairs significantly different Size difference pair Adjusted p-values (extra conservative) Confidence intervals difference

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Adjusted P-values: Extra conservative prevent false positives p < 0.05: Groups significantly different (can confident) p ≥ 0.05: significant difference groups Scheffe adjustments stricter methods Confidence Intervals: Wider Tukey's include 0: Groups differ significantly Include 0: significant difference Wider intervals reflect extra caution

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Use Scheffe test : ANOVA shows significant differences (p < 0.05) Group sizes unequal want extra cautious false positives might test complex comparisons (just pairs) Sample sizes small

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Scheffe Test: conservative (hardest find differences) Best unequal group sizes Protects possible comparisons Wider confidence intervals Tukey Test: Less conservative (easier find differences) Best equal group sizes Protects pairwise comparisons Narrower confidence intervals

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Example: "Group - Group B: Diff = 3.2, p = 0.082" Group 's average 3.2 units higher Group B's difference significant Scheffe (p > 0.05) might significant less conservative tests

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Scheffe may find differences even ANOVA normal - extra careful Consider Tukey group sizes similar Report post-hoc test used Focus confidence intervals, just p-values

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Scheffe, H. (1953). method judging contrasts analysis variance. Biometrika, 40(1-2), 87-110. Scheffe, H. (1959). Analysis Variance. New York: Wiley.

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Perform ANOVA followed by Scheffe post-hoc test anova_result <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education)  # Scheffe post-hoc comparisons anova_result %>% scheffe_test() #> Scheffe Post-Hoc Test Results #> ----------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Family-wise error rate controlled using Scheffe's method #>   Note: Most conservative post-hoc test (widest confidence intervals) #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Scheffe Results: #> ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                                   Comparison Difference Lower CI Upper CI #>     Basic Secondary - Intermediate Secondary     -0.497   -0.662   -0.331 #>         Basic Secondary - Academic Secondary     -0.649   -0.816   -0.483 #>                 Basic Secondary - University     -0.843   -1.034   -0.651 #>  Intermediate Secondary - Academic Secondary     -0.153   -0.330    0.024 #>          Intermediate Secondary - University     -0.346   -0.547   -0.145 #>              Academic Secondary - University     -0.193   -0.395    0.009 #>  p-value Sig #>    <.001 *** #>    <.001 *** #>    <.001 *** #>    0.121     #>    <.001 *** #>    0.067     #> ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive differences: First group > Second group #> - Negative differences: First group < Second group #> - Confidence intervals not containing 0 indicate significant differences #> - p-values are adjusted for all possible contrasts (most conservative) #> - Scheffe test has wider CIs than Tukey HSD  # Multiple variables anova_result_multi <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, income, group = education)  anova_result_multi %>% scheffe_test() #> Scheffe Post-Hoc Test Results #> ----------------------------- #>  #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Family-wise error rate controlled using Scheffe's method #>   Note: Most conservative post-hoc test (widest confidence intervals) #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Scheffe Results: #> ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                                   Comparison Difference Lower CI Upper CI #>     Basic Secondary - Intermediate Secondary     -0.497   -0.662   -0.331 #>         Basic Secondary - Academic Secondary     -0.649   -0.816   -0.483 #>                 Basic Secondary - University     -0.843   -1.034   -0.651 #>  Intermediate Secondary - Academic Secondary     -0.153   -0.330    0.024 #>          Intermediate Secondary - University     -0.346   -0.547   -0.145 #>              Academic Secondary - University     -0.193   -0.395    0.009 #>  p-value Sig #>    <.001 *** #>    <.001 *** #>    <.001 *** #>    0.121     #>    <.001 *** #>    0.067     #> ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> --- income --- #>  #> Scheffe Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                                   Comparison Difference  Lower CI  Upper CI #>     Basic Secondary - Intermediate Secondary   -833.471 -1010.178  -656.763 #>         Basic Secondary - Academic Secondary  -1465.040 -1641.748 -1288.332 #>                 Basic Secondary - University  -2578.135 -2780.499 -2375.772 #>  Intermediate Secondary - Academic Secondary   -631.569  -820.717  -442.422 #>          Intermediate Secondary - University  -1744.665 -1957.977 -1531.353 #>              Academic Secondary - University  -1113.096 -1326.408  -899.783 #>  p-value Sig #>    <.001 *** #>    <.001 *** #>    <.001 *** #>    <.001 *** #>    <.001 *** #>    <.001 *** #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive differences: First group > Second group #> - Negative differences: First group < Second group #> - Confidence intervals not containing 0 indicate significant differences #> - p-values are adjusted for all possible contrasts (most conservative) #> - Scheffe test has wider CIs than Tukey HSD  # Weighted analysis anova_weighted <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education, weights = sampling_weight)  anova_weighted %>% scheffe_test() #> Weighted Scheffe Post-Hoc Test Results #> -------------------------------------- #>  #> - title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: Calculates Spearman's rank correlation coefficients (rho) variables support weighted correlations, grouped data, multiple variable pairs. Provides significance testing SPSS-compatible output formatting. Spearman's rho non-parametric measure rank correlation assesses monotonic relationships variables. particularly suitable ordinal data assumptions Pearson correlation met.

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
code: spearman_rho(   data,   ...,   weights = NULL,   alternative = c("two.sided", "less", "greater"),   use = c("pairwise", "listwise"),   na.rm = NULL )

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... variables want correlate. List two single correlation correlation matrix. can use helpers like starts_with("trust"). weights Optional survey weights population-representative results. Note: SPSS may apply weights Spearman's rho; implementation uses weighted ranks mathematically correct survey analysis. alternative Direction test: "two.sided" (default): Two-tailed test "less": One-tailed test (negative correlation) "greater": One-tailed test (positive correlation) use handle missing values: "pairwise" (default): Pairwise deletion - correlation uses available cases "listwise": Listwise deletion - complete cases across variables na.rm Deprecated. Use use instead.

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: Correlation results showing rank-based relationships variables, including rho coefficient, p-value, t-statistic, sample size pair. multiple variables, correlation, significance, sample size matrices also provided.

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: Spearman's rho measures strength direction monotonic relationship two variables. Unlike Pearson correlation, require linear relationship – just needs one variable consistently increase (decrease) increases. Think ranking data first, checking ranks tend go together. rho value ranges -1 +1: Strong positive (0.7 1.0): High ranks one variable go high ranks Moderate positive (0.3 0.7): Moderate tendency ranks increase together Weak positive (0 0.3): Slight tendency ranks increase together correlation (near 0): relationship variables Negative values: one variable's rank increases, 's tends decrease output also provides: p-value: Probability seeing correlation chance n: Number observation pairs used significance stars: Visual indicator (*** strong, ** strong, * moderate evidence)

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: Choose Spearman's rho : relationship monotonic necessarily linear data outliers (less impact ranks) variables ordinal (ordered categories) sure data meets Pearson correlation assumptions want detect monotonic trend, just linear ones

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: Spearman's rho usually larger magnitude Kendall's tau Spearman's rho better detecting linear relationships ranks Kendall's tau robust better statistical properties Spearman's rho commonly reported research

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: Spearman, C. (1904). proof measurement association two things. American Journal Psychology, 15(1), 72–101. Lehmann, E. L. (1975). Nonparametrics: Statistical Methods Based Ranks. Holden-Day.

Method: Spearman's rho (rank correlation) #> - Variables: life_satisfaction, political_orientation #> - Missing data handling: pairwise deletion #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #>  #> --- life_satisfaction × political_orientation --- #>  #>   Spearman's rho: ρ = -0.004 #>   t-statistic: -0.210 #>   Sample size: n = 2228 #>   p-value (2-tailed): 0.8334 #>   Significance: ns #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: weak negative monotonic relationship  # Correlation matrix for multiple variables survey_data %>%   spearman_rho(life_satisfaction, political_orientation, trust_media) #>  #> Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis  #> ------------------------------------- #>  #> - what: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: t_test() helps determine two groups different average values. example, men women different satisfaction scores? Region spend Region B? year better last year? test tells : Whether difference statistically significant big difference (effect sizes) likely range true difference (confidence interval)

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
code: t_test(   data,   ...,   group = NULL,   weights = NULL,   var.equal = FALSE,   mu = 0,   alternative = c("two.sided", "less", "greater"),   conf.level = 0.95 )

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables test. List multiple variables use tidyselect helpers like starts_with("trust"). group variable defines two groups (e.g., gender, region). Must exactly two categories. Leave empty one-sample tests. weights Optional survey weights population-representative results var.equal assume equal variances? (Default: FALSE) FALSE: Safer, works even groups vary differently (Welch's test) TRUE: Traditional approach, assumes equal spread (Student's test) mu Comparison value (Default: 0). two-sample tests, usually 0. one-sample tests, benchmark value. alternative Direction test: "two.sided" (default): difference "greater": First group higher "less": First group lower conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95%)

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: Test results showing whether groups differ, including: t-statistic p-value statistical significance Mean difference confidence interval Effect sizes (Cohen's d, Hedges' g, Glass' Delta) Group statistics (mean, SD, sample size)

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: P-value: difference statistically significant? p < 0.001: strong evidence difference p < 0.01: Strong evidence difference p < 0.05: Moderate evidence difference p ≥ 0.05: significant difference found Effect Sizes (big difference?): Cohen's d: standard measure |d| < 0.2: Negligible difference |d| = 0.2-0.5: Small difference |d| = 0.5-0.8: Medium difference |d| > 0.8: Large difference Hedges' g: Corrected small samples Glass' Delta: Uses control group SD Confidence Interval: Range true difference likely falls includes 0, groups may differ Width indicates precision (narrower = precise)

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: Use t-test : exactly two groups compare outcome variable numeric (continuous) Groups independent (different people ) Data roughly normally distributed (n ≥ 30 per group) use : two groups (use ANOVA) Data severely skewed small samples (use Mann-Whitney) Groups paired/matched (use paired t-test - coming soon) Variables categorical (use chi-square)

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: results show variance assumptions: Welch's test (var.equal = FALSE): Safer, assume equal variances Student's test (var.equal = TRUE): Traditional, assumes equal variances result t = 2.45, p = 0.015, d = 0.62 means: Groups 2.45 standard errors apart (t-statistic) 1.5% chance due random variation (p-value) Medium-sized practical difference (Cohen's d)

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: Always check sample sizes (aim 30+ per group) Consider statistical significance effect size Use weights population-level conclusions Plot data first check assumptions Report confidence intervals along p-values

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical Power Analysis Behavioral Sciences (2nd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum Associates. Hedges, L. V. (1981). Distribution theory Glass's estimator effect size related estimators. Journal Educational Statistics, 6(2), 107-128. Glass, G. V. (1976). Primary, secondary, meta-analysis research. Educational Researcher, 5(10), 3-8. Welch, B. L. (1947). generalization "Student's" problem several different population variances involved. Biometrika, 34(1-2), 28-35.

what: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: tukey_test() tells exactly groups different ANOVA finds overall differences. like follow-investigation pinpoints differences lie. Think : ANOVA says "differences somewhere" Tukey test says "specifically, Group differs Group C" way make possible comparisons controlling error rates

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
code: tukey_test(x, conf.level = 0.95, ...)  # Default S3 method tukey_test(x, conf.level = 0.95, ...)

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: x ANOVA results oneway_anova() conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95%) ... Additional arguments (currently unused)

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: Pairwise comparison results showing: group pairs significantly different Size difference pair Adjusted p-values (controlling multiple comparisons) Confidence intervals difference

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: Adjusted P-values: Control multiple comparisons p < 0.05: Groups significantly different p ≥ 0.05: significant difference groups make many comparisons, chance alone produce false positives Tukey adjustment protects conservative Mean Differences: Positive: First group higher average second Negative: Second group higher average first Zero confidence interval: significant difference

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: Use Tukey test : ANOVA shows significant differences (p < 0.05) want know specific groups differ need compare possible pairs Group sizes roughly equal Variances roughly equal across groups

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: Tukey Test: Less conservative (easier find differences) Best equal group sizes Protects pairwise comparisons Narrower confidence intervals Scheffe Test: conservative (hardest find differences) Best unequal group sizes Protects possible comparisons Wider confidence intervals

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: Example: "Group - Group B: Diff = 3.2, p = 0.012" Group 's average 3.2 units higher Group B's difference statistically significant (p < 0.05) can confident groups truly differ

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: run post-hoc tests ANOVA significant Focus comparisons make theoretical sense Consider practical significance, just statistical Report difference confidence interval Remember: non-significant mean "exactly equal"

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: Tukey, J. W. (1949). Comparing individual means analysis variance. Biometrics, 5(2), 99-114. Kramer, C. Y. (1956). Extension multiple range tests group means unequal numbers replications. Biometrics, 12(3), 307-310.

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test

education)  # Tukey post-hoc comparisons anova_result %>% tukey_test() #> Tukey HSD Post-Hoc Test Results #> ------------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Family-wise error rate controlled using Tukey HSD #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Tukey Results: #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                                 Comparison Difference Lower CI Upper CI p-value #>     Intermediate Secondary-Basic Secondary      0.497    0.344    0.649   <.001 #>         Academic Secondary-Basic Secondary      0.649    0.496    0.802   <.001 #>                 University-Basic Secondary              University-Academic Secondary      0.193    0.008    0.379   0.037 #>  Sig #>  *** #>  *** #>  *** #>      #>  *** #>    * #> group > Second group #> - Negative differences: First group < Second group #> - Confidence intervals not containing 0 indicate significant differences #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons (family-wise error control)  # With weights anova_weighted <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education, weights = sampling_weight)  anova_weighted %>% title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: w_iqr() calculates interquartile range using survey weights. IQR distance 25th 75th percentiles – tells range contains middle 50% population. Unlike standard deviation, IQR affected outliers, making robust measure spread.

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
code: w_iqr(data, ..., weights = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables want analyze. can list multiple variables use helpers like starts_with("income") weights Survey weights make results representative population. Without weights, get simple sample IQR. na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE)

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: Population-weighted IQR(s) sample size information, including weighted IQR, effective sample size (effective N), number valid observations used.

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: Weighted IQR: range covers middle 50% weighted population. larger IQR means spread central part data. Effective N: many independent observations weighted data represents. N: actual number observations used. IQR especially useful data skewed. example, income data, IQR gives better sense "typical spread" SD extreme incomes distort .

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: Use w_iqr() : data outliers skewed (e.g., income, response times) want robust measure spread influenced extremes need describe spread middle 50% population need SPSS-compatible weighted IQR values

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: \(IQR_w = Q_{3,w} - Q_{1,w}\) \(Q_{1,w}\) \(Q_{3,w}\) weighted 25th 75th percentiles, calculated using cumulative weights (see w_quantile).

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
