title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis
text: w_kurtosis() measures heavy tails data's distribution , using survey weights population-representative results. Kurtosis tells whether data unusually many extreme values (outliers) compared normal distribution: Positive (excess) kurtosis: Heavier tails normal (outliers) Negative (excess) kurtosis: Lighter tails normal (fewer outliers) Near zero: Similar tail behavior normal distribution

Without weights, get simple sample kurtosis. na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE) excess Show excess kurtosis? (Default: TRUE, matching SPSS). Excess kurtosis subtracts 3 normal distribution kurtosis 0, making interpretation easier.

title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis
text: Population-weighted kurtosis value(s) sample size information, including weighted kurtosis, effective sample size (effective N), number valid observations used.

title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis
text: Weighted Kurtosis (excess, default): Near 0: Tail behavior similar normal distribution Positive (> 0): Heavier tails, extreme values normal Negative (< 0): Lighter tails, fewer extreme values normal Values beyond 2 -2 indicate notable departure normality Effective N: many independent observations weighted data represents. N: actual number observations used. Kurtosis often checked together skewness assess normality. close zero normally distributed data.

title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis

title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis
text: Uses SPSS Type 2 (sample-corrected) excess kurtosis formula. First, population excess kurtosis (\(g_2\)) calculated: \(g_2 = \frac{m_4}{m_2^2} - 3\) \(m_2 = \sum w_i(x_i - \bar{x}_w)^2 / V_1\) \(m_4 = \sum w_i(x_i - \bar{x}_w)^4 / V_1\) \(V_1 = \sum w_i\). , bias-corrected (Type 2) kurtosis : \(G_2 = \frac{(n+1) \cdot g_2 + 6}{(n-2)(n-3)} \cdot (n-1)\) \(n = V_1\) weighted data.

text: Weighted SE: precision weighted mean estimate. Smaller values mean precise estimates. can build 95% confidence interval : weighted mean +/- 1.96 * weighted SE. Effective N: many independent observations weighted data represents. Weights vary lot reduce effective N, increasing SE. N: actual number observations used.

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Errors — w_se
text: Use w_se() : need report precision mean estimates want construct confidence intervals weighted means need compare precision across subgroups need SPSS-compatible weighted standard errors

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Errors — w_se
text: weighted standard error calculated : \(SE_w = \frac{s_w}{\sqrt{V_1}}\) \(s_w\) weighted standard deviation (see w_sd) \(V_1 = \sum w_i\) sum weights. unweighted case: \(SE = s / \sqrt{n}\)

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Errors — w_se
text: IBM Corp. (2023). IBM SPSS Statistics 29 Algorithms. IBM Corporation.

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Errors — w_se
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic weighted standard error survey_data %>% w_se(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Standard Error Statistics #> ---------------------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_se Effective_N #>       age       0.341      2468.8 #>   # Multiple variables survey_data %>% w_se(age, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Standard Error Statistics #> ---------------------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_se Effective_N #>       age       0.341      2468.8 #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_se Effective_N #>    income      30.353      2158.9 #>   # Grouped data survey_data %>% group_by(region) %>% w_se(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Standard Error Statistics #> ---------------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = East #> Warning: Unknown or uninitialised column: `Variable`. #>  #> Group: region = West #> Warning: Unknown or uninitialised column: `Variable`. #>   # In summarise context survey_data %>% summarise(se_age = w_se(age, weights = sampling_weight)) #> # A tibble: 1 × 1 #>   se_age #>    <dbl> #> 1  0.341  # Unweighted (for comparison) survey_data %>% w_se(age) #>  #> Standard Error Statistics #> ------------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable   se    N #>       age 0.34 2500 #>

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: w_skew() measures asymmetric data's distribution , using survey weights population-representative results. Skewness tells whether values tend trail left right: Positive skew: long tail right (e.g., income – many low, high) Negative skew: long tail left (e.g., exam scores – many high, low) Near zero: Roughly symmetric (e.g., height population)

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
code: w_skew(data, ..., weights = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables want analyze. can list multiple variables use helpers like starts_with("trust") weights Survey weights make results representative population. Without weights, get simple sample skewness. na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE)

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: Population-weighted skewness value(s) sample size information, including weighted skewness, effective sample size (effective N), number valid observations used.

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: Weighted Skewness: population-representative skewness value. Near 0: Distribution roughly symmetric -0.5 0.5: Approximately symmetric -1 -0.5 0.5 1: Moderately skewed Beyond -1 1: Highly skewed Effective N: many independent observations weighted data represents. N: actual number observations used. High skewness suggests might want use median instead mean measure center, non-parametric tests instead t-tests.

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: Use w_skew() : need check variable normally distributed want decide parametric non-parametric tests need assess whether mean good summary measure need SPSS-compatible weighted skewness values

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: Uses SPSS Type 2 (sample-corrected) skewness formula. First, population skewness (\(g_1\)) calculated: \(g_1 = \frac{m_3}{m_2^{3/2}}\) \(m_2 = \sum w_i(x_i - \bar{x}_w)^2 / V_1\) \(m_3 = \sum w_i(x_i - \bar{x}_w)^3 / V_1\) \(V_1 = \sum w_i\). , bias-corrected (Type 2) skewness : \(G_1 = g_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n(n-1)}}{n-2}\) \(n = V_1\) weighted data.

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: Joanes, D. N., & Gill, C. . (1998). Comparing measures sample skewness kurtosis. Statistician, 47(1), 183-189. IBM Corp. (2023). IBM SPSS Statistics 29 Algorithms. IBM Corporation.

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic weighted skewness survey_data %>% w_skew(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Skewness Statistics #> ---------------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_skew Effective_N #>       age         0.159      2468.8 #>   # Multiple variables survey_data %>% w_skew(age, income, life_satisfaction, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Skewness Statistics #> ---------------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_skew Effective_N #>       age         0.159      2468.8 #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_skew Effective_N #>    income         0.725      2158.9 #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>           Variable weighted_skew Effective_N #>  life_satisfaction        -0.499      2390.9 #>   # Grouped data survey_data %>% group_by(region) %>% w_skew(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Skewness Statistics #> ---------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = East #> Warning: Unknown or uninitialised column: `Variable`. #>  #> Group: region = West #> Warning: Unknown or uninitialised column: `Variable`. #>   # In summarise context survey_data %>% summarise(skew_age = w_skew(age, weights = sampling_weight)) #> # A tibble: 1 × 1 #>   skew_age #>      <dbl> #> 1    0.159  # Unweighted (for comparison) survey_data %>% w_skew(age) #>  #> Skewness Statistics #> ------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable  skew    N #>       age 0.172 2500 #>

title: Calculate Population-Representative Variance — w_var
text: w_var() calculates variance accurately represents population using survey weights. Variance measures far values spread average – square standard deviation. larger variance means dispersion population's responses. Without weights, describe spread sample . weights, estimate spread values entire population.

title: Calculate Population-Representative Variance — w_var
code: w_var(data, ..., weights = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Calculate Population-Representative Variance — w_var
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables want analyze. can list multiple variables use helpers like starts_with("trust") weights Survey weights make results representative population. Without weights, get simple sample variance. na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE)

title: Calculate Population-Representative Variance — w_var
text: Population-weighted variance(s) sample size information, including weighted variance, effective sample size (effective N), number valid observations used.

title: Calculate Population-Representative Variance — w_var
text: Weighted Variance: population-representative variance. variance squared units (e.g., years-squared age), often easier interpret standard deviation (w_sd) instead. Effective N: many independent observations weighted data represents. Always less equal actual sample size. N: actual number observations used calculation.

title: Calculate Population-Representative Variance — w_var
text: Use w_var() : need variance calculations (e.g., pooled variance, F-tests) want compare variability across groups proper weighting analysis formulas require variance rather SD need SPSS-compatible weighted variance values reporting purposes, w_sd usually preferred standard deviation units original variable.

title: Calculate Population-Representative Variance — w_var
text: weighted variance uses SPSS frequency weights formula: \(s^2_w = \frac{\sum w_i (x_i - \bar{x}_w)^2}{V_1 - 1}\) \(V_1 = \sum w_i\) sum weights \(\bar{x}_w = \sum w_i x_i / V_1\) weighted mean. Note: \(s^2_w = (s_w)^2\), .e., weighted variance square weighted standard deviation.

title: Calculate Population-Representative Variance — w_var
text: IBM Corp. (2023). IBM SPSS Statistics 29 Algorithms. IBM Corporation.

title: Calculate Population-Representative Variance — w_var
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic weighted variance survey_data %>% w_var(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Variance Statistics #> ---------------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_var Effective_N #>       age      291.857      2468.8 #>   # Multiple variables survey_data %>% w_var(age, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Variance Statistics #> ---------------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_var Effective_N #>       age      291.857      2468.8 #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_var Effective_N #>    income      2027678      2158.9 #>   # Grouped data survey_data %>% group_by(region) %>% w_var(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Variance Statistics #> ---------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = East #> Warning: Unknown or uninitialised column: `Variable`. #>  #> Group: region = West #> Warning: Unknown or uninitialised column: `Variable`. #>   # In summarise context survey_data %>% summarise(var_age = w_var(age, weights = sampling_weight)) #> # A tibble: 1 × 1 #>   var_age #>     <dbl> #> 1    292.  # Unweighted (for comparison) survey_data %>% w_var(age) #>  #> Variance Statistics #> ------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable     var    N #>       age 288.185 2500 #>

what: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
title: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
text: wilcoxon_test() compares two paired measurements subjects data normally distributed. non-parametric alternative paired t-test. Think : way test whether scores changed two time points Comparing ratings two items respondents robust paired comparison works data shape test tells : Whether scores significantly different two measurements many subjects increased, decreased, stayed strength effect (effect size r)

title: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
code: wilcoxon_test(data, x, y, weights = NULL, conf.level = 0.95)

title: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) wide format, one row per subject two measurements separate columns x first measurement variable (e.g., pre-test, trust government) y second measurement variable (e.g., post-test, trust media). difference computed y - x weights Optional survey weights population-representative results conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95 percent)

title: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
text: Test results showing whether two measurements differ, including: Z statistic (standardized test statistic, normal approximation) P-value (significant difference?) Effect size r (strong difference?) Rank statistics (negative ranks, positive ranks, ties)

title: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
text: P-value: p < 0.05, two measurements significantly different p < 0.001: strong evidence difference p < 0.01: Strong evidence difference p < 0.05: Moderate evidence difference p > 0.05: significant difference found Effect Size r (strong difference?): < 0.1: Negligible effect 0.1 - 0.3: Small effect 0.3 - 0.5: Medium effect 0.5 higher: Large effect Rank Categories: Negative Ranks: subjects y < x (decreased) Positive Ranks: subjects y > x (increased) Ties: subjects y = x (change)

title: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
text: Use Wilcoxon signed-rank test : Comparing two related measurements subjects data normally distributed ordinal data (ratings, rankings) Sample size small want robust alternative paired t-test

title: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
text: normally distributed paired data: Use paired t-test instead independent groups: Use mann_whitney() instead 3+ related measurements: Use friedman_test() instead

title: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
text: Wilcoxon, F. (1945). Individual comparisons ranking methods. Biometrics Bulletin, 1(6), 80-83. Fritz, C. O., Morris, P. E., & Richler, J. J. (2012). Effect size estimates: current use, calculations, interpretation. Journal Experimental Psychology: General, 141(1), 2.

Pair: trust_media vs trust_government #>  #> ── trust_media - trust_government ────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #>   Ranks: #>   ------------------------------------------- #>                      N Mean Rank Sum of Ranks #>    Negative Ranks  955    887.53     847592.5 #>    Positive Ranks  770    832.57     641082.5 #>              Ties  502        NA           NA #>             Total 2227        NA           NA #>   ------------------------------------------- #>  #>   a trust_media < trust_government #>   b trust_media > trust_government #>   c trust_media = trust_government #>  #>   Test Statistics: #>   ---------------------------- #>         Z p value Effect r sig #>    -5.097       0    0.123 *** #>   ---------------------------- #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation (r): #> - title: mariposa 0.5.0
text: Added factorial_anova() multi-factor -subjects ANOVA (3 factors) Type III Sum Squares matching SPSS UNIANOVA. Includes main effects, interaction terms, partial eta squared, R-squared, Levene’s test homogeneity variance. Full survey weight support via WLS (matching SPSS /REGWGT). Integrates existing tukey_test(), scheffe_test(), levene_test() S3 generics. Added ancova() Analysis Covariance — tests group differences controlling continuous covariates. Matches SPSS UNIANOVA keyword. Provides ANOVA table, parameter estimates (B, SE, t, p, partial eta squared), estimated marginal means (adjusted covariates), Levene’s test. Supports 3 factors multiple covariates full survey weight support.

title: mariposa 0.5.0
text: Added 612 SPSS validation tests factorial_anova() across 9 scenarios: unweighted (2-factor, 3-factor, 2-factor missing data), weighted (2-factor, 3-factor), grouped (2-factor, 3-factor), weighted+grouped (2-factor, 3-factor). Added 579 SPSS validation tests ancova() across 11 scenarios: one-way ANCOVA, two-way ANCOVA, weighted, grouped, weighted+grouped, multiple covariates, single factor single covariate. Total test suite: 4,986 tests passing (0 failures).

title: mariposa 0.5.0
text: Type III Sum Squares computed via contr.sum contrasts stats::drop1() — dependency car package. Weighted analyses use WLS (stats::lm() weights), matching SPSS’s /REGWGT subcommand behavior exactly. Weighted Levene’s test uses SPSS /REGWGT algorithm: z_i = sqrt(w_i) * |y_i - weighted_cell_mean_i| followed unweighted ANOVA. Corrected Model SS computed Corrected Total - Error (sum Type III SS) correctly handle unbalanced designs.

title: mariposa 0.4.0
text: Added fisher_test() Fisher’s exact test independence contingency tables. Recommended sample sizes small expected cell frequencies fall 5 (chi-square approximation becomes unreliable). Supports survey weights, multi-variable analysis, group_by(). Added chisq_gof() chi-square goodness--fit testing. Tests whether observed frequency distribution categorical variable matches expected distribution (default: equal proportions). Supports custom expected proportions, residual analysis, survey weights, multi-variable analysis. Added mcnemar_test() testing changes paired proportions two dichotomous measurements (e.g., /designs). Provides asymptotic exact binomial p-values, 2×2 contingency tables, continuity correction. Supports survey weights. Added dunn_test() S3 generic Dunn’s post-hoc pairwise comparisons following significant Kruskal-Wallis test. Identifies specific group pairs differ using rank-based Z-statistics adjustable p-value correction (Bonferroni, Holm, BH, etc.). Dispatches kruskal_wallis result objects. Added pairwise_wilcoxon() S3 generic pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank post-hoc comparisons following significant Friedman test. Identifies measurement pairs differ adjustable p-value correction. Dispatches friedman_test result objects.

title: mariposa 0.4.0
text: Added SPSS validation tests 5 new functions across weighted/unweighted grouped/ungrouped scenarios.

title: mariposa 0.4.0
text: Extended post-hoc analysis framework: dunn_test() pairwise_wilcoxon() join tukey_test(), scheffe_test(), levene_test() S3 generics dispatch parent test result objects.

title: mariposa 0.3.1
text: efa() now supports Maximum Likelihood (ML) extraction via extraction = "ml". ML extraction provides goodness--fit chi-square test, initial communalities SMC (squared multiple correlations), uniquenesses. Uses stats::factanal() correlation matrix input seamless survey weight support. efa() now supports Promax rotation via rotation = "promax". Like Oblimin, Promax oblique rotation produces Pattern Matrix, Structure Matrix, Factor Correlation Matrix. Uses stats::promax() (base R, new dependency). Internal refactoring efa(): extraction logic separated .efa_extract_pca() .efa_extract_ml() cleaner architecture easier extension future extraction methods (PAF planned).

title: mariposa 0.3.0
text: Added kruskal_wallis() comparing 3+ independent groups ordinal data (non-parametric alternative one-way ANOVA). Supports survey weights, group_by(), multi-variable analysis. Effect size: Eta-squared. Added wilcoxon_test() comparing two paired measurements without assuming normality (Wilcoxon signed-rank test). Includes rank categories (negative, positive, ties) effect size r. Added friedman_test() comparing 3+ related measurements ordinal data (non-parametric alternative repeated-measures ANOVA). Effect size: Kendall’s W. Added binomial_test() testing whether observed proportion matches expected value (exact binomial test). Supports multiple binary variables custom test proportions.

title: mariposa 0.3.0
text: Added 294 new SPSS validation tests across 4 non-parametric functions, covering weighted/unweighted grouped/ungrouped scenarios. Total test suite: 2,227 tests passing (0 failures, 0 skips).

title: mariposa 0.2.0
text: Added reliability() Cronbach’s Alpha item statistics, including corrected item-total correlations, alpha--item-deleted, inter-item correlation matrix. Genuine implementation full survey weight support. Added efa() Exploratory Factor Analysis PCA extraction. Supports Varimax rotation (Base R) Oblimin rotation (via optional GPArotation package). Includes KMO measure, Bartlett’s test, communalities, sorted factor loading matrix configurable blank threshold. Added scale_index() creating mean indices across survey items, min_valid parameter matching SPSS MEAN.x() syntax. Designed use inside dplyr::mutate(). Added pomps() Percent Maximum Possible Scores transformation, rescaling values 0-100 range cross-scale comparability. Added linear_regression() wrapper around stats::lm() SPSS-compatible output: coefficients table (B, SE, Beta, t, p), ANOVA table, model summary (R, R-squared, adjusted R-squared), standardized coefficients. Supports formula SPSS-style (dependent/predictors) interfaces. Added logistic_regression() wrapper around stats::glm() odds ratios, Wald statistics, pseudo-R-squared measures (Nagelkerke, Cox-Snell, McFadden), classification table.

title: mariposa 0.2.0
text: Added GPArotation suggested dependency Oblimin rotation efa(). Added MASS suggested dependency enhanced regression diagnostics.

title: mariposa 0.2.0
text: 6 new functions support survey weights grouped analysis via dplyr::group_by(). functions include comprehensive roxygen2 documentation practical examples, “Use” guidance, “Understanding Output” sections.

title: mariposa 0.1.0
text: gamma() renamed goodman_gamma() avoid shadowing base::gamma(). function remains alias chi_square() works identically. S3 class names unified: removed _results suffix result classes (e.g., chi_square_results -> chi_square, t_test_results -> t_test). Class names now match function name created .

title: mariposa 0.1.0
text: Fixed namespace collisions triple-defined internal helper functions (.process_variables(), .process_weights(), .effective_n()). now defined helpers.R shared across functions. Fixed weighted variance/SD formula inconsistency. weighted calculations now use SPSS frequency weights formula: sum(w * (x - w_mean)^2) / (V1 - 1). Fixed Gamma ASE (asymptotic standard error) calculation. Replaced empirical magic-number formula correct ASE0 formula Agresti (2002). Fixed weighted Cohen’s d calculation t_test(). Previously multiplied values weights (x * w); now uses proper weighted means pooled weighted standard deviation. Fixed weighted kurtosis formula. Changed population excess kurtosis (m4/m2^2 - 3) SPSS Type 2 sample-corrected formula (G2 = ((n+1)*g2 + 6) * (n-1) / ((n-2)*(n-3))), matching SPSS output.

title: mariposa 0.1.0
text: Refactored 9 11 w_* functions use shared factory pattern (R/w_factory.R). Eliminated ~2,460 lines duplicated boilerplate (4,204 → 1,740 lines, -58.6%). w_modus w_quantile remain standalone due fundamentally different interfaces. Added 83 SPSS validation tests w_* functions across 4 scenarios (weighted/unweighted × grouped/ungrouped) test-weighted-statistics-spss-validation.R. Reduced memory usage: result objects now store columns needed post-hoc tests instead full input data frame. Unified print helper system: output formatting now uses print_helpers.R. Removed deprecated .print_header(), .print_border(), .get_border(), .print_group_header() helpers.R. Deduplicated t_test.R print methods: print.t_test_results print.t_test_result now share common implementation (~360 fewer lines). Added input validation: t_test(): validates conf.level 0 1 t_test(), oneway_anova(): validate selected variables numeric chi_square(): warns expected cell counts < 5 Migrated error handling stop()/warning() cli_abort()/cli_warn() structured messages, {.arg} {.var} markup, pluralization support. Added cli dependency professional user-facing messages print output. Added @family tags 24 exported functions cross-referencing documentation (families: descriptive, hypothesis_tests, correlation, posthoc, weighted_statistics). Added tests/testthat/helper-mariposa.R shared test utilities centralized SPSS validation tolerances. Migrated print_helpers.R infrastructure cli (cli_rule(), cli_bullets(), cli_h2()). Extended globals.R missing NSE variable declarations. Fixed “SURVEYSTAT” reference imports.R.
